License: arXiv.org perpetual non-exclusive license
arXiv:2604.26196v1 [math.SG] 29 Apr 2026

Concurring reduction schemes for Dirac structures

Dan Aguero, Alessandro Arsie,
Pedro Frejlich, and Igor Mencattini
Abstract.

The notion of concurrence was recently proposed as the natural compatibility relation between Dirac structures, generalizing the commutativity of two Poisson structures. We address the question of when a reduction scheme — that is, a way to induce a Dirac structure on a quotient of a submanifold — respects this relation. After characterizing the minimal scheme of Dirac reduction, we prove that two concurring Dirac structures have concurring reductions whenever they share a common witness, extending to Dirac geometry the reduction of the Marsden-Ra t , iu theorem.

Two procedures for constructing such common witnesses are given, the second being the Dirac counterpart of Magri’s original recipe in bihamiltonian geometry. Examples drawn from Hamiltonian actions, Dirac-Nijenhuis manifolds, and complex Dirac structures conclude the paper and illustrate our methods.

Contents
  1. 1 Introduction
  2. 2 Preliminaries
  3. 3 Induced and projectable Dirac structures
  4. 4 Dirac reduction
  5. 5 Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction
  6. 6 Witnesses of concurrent reduction
  7. 7 Compatibility with concurrence
  8. 8 Reduction by kernels
  9. 9 Magri’s recipe
  10. 10 Further comments and examples
  11. References

1. Introduction

Noether’s principle states that continuous symmetries of a system have a corresponding conservation law. The knowledge that a certain quantity is conserved along trajectories of the system often allows one to “reduce the degrees of freedom” of the system, in the sense that the description of the trajectories of the system can be described in terms of a smaller space — namely, the orbits among those points in the phase space which share the same conserved quantities as the initial condition.

In Symplectic Geometry, the principle above takes the form of the theorem of Marsden-Weinstein-Meyer [1, 2] that, if a symplectic manifold has a Hamiltonian action of a Lie group GG,

G↷(M,ωM)⟶μ𝔤∗,\displaystyle G\curvearrowright(M,\omega_{M})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mu}}{{\longrightarrow}}\mathfrak{g}^{*},

then111“Under favorable circumstances” or “modulo technical details”: for the sake of readability, we will not emphasize in this introduction technical details (typically demanding constant rank or connectedness conditions), and we refer the reader to the body of the text for complete statements. the symplectic structure ωM\omega_{M} on MM reduces to a symplectic form on YY,

(1) i∗​(ωM)=p∗​(ωY),\displaystyle i^{*}(\omega_{M})=p^{*}(\omega_{Y}),

where

M⟵iX:=μ−1​(c)⟶pY:=X/Gc\displaystyle M\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i}}{{\longleftarrow}}X:=\mu^{-1}(c)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}Y:=X/G_{c}

and i:X→Mi:X\to M denotes the inclusion, GcG_{c} the isotropy subgroup of cc, and p:X→Yp:X\to Y the quotient map. In the general Poisson setting, in which ωM\omega_{M} is replaced by a Poisson structure πM\pi_{M}, the analogue of the reduction relation (1) above can be written as follows: there is a Poisson structure πY\pi_{Y} on YY, uniquely defined by the condition that, whenever we are given functions f,g∈C∞​(Y)f,g\in C^{\infty}(Y) and F,G∈C∞​(M)F,G\in C^{\infty}(M) with i∗​(F)=p∗​(f)i^{*}(F)=p^{*}(f) and i∗​(G)=p∗​(g)i^{*}(G)=p^{*}(g), then

(2) i∗​{F,G}πM=p∗​{f,g}πY.\displaystyle i^{*}\{F,G\}^{\pi_{M}}=p^{*}\{f,g\}^{\pi_{Y}}.

Condition (2) can be written more succinctly in the language of Dirac geometry [6, 21, 27] — a generalization of Poisson geometry in which it is meaningful to consider the pullback of a Poisson structure by a smooth map. Briefly, a Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM is a vector subbundle LM⊂𝕋​M:=T​M⊕T∗​ML_{M}\subset\mathbb{T}M:=TM\oplus T^{*}M of the generalized tangent bundle 𝕋​M\mathbb{T}M of MM, which is isotropic for the canonical symmetric pairing

⟨u+ξ,v+η⟩:=⟨u,η⟩+⟨ξ,v⟩,\displaystyle\langle u+\xi,v+\eta\rangle:=\langle u,\eta\rangle+\langle\xi,v\rangle,

and whose space of sections Γ​(LM)\Gamma(L_{M}) is involutive under the Dorfman bracket

(3) [u+ξ,v+η]=[u,v]+ℒu​η−ιv​d​ξ.\displaystyle[u+\xi,v+\eta]=[u,v]+\mathscr{L}_{u}\eta-\iota_{v}\mathrm{d}\xi.

The prototypical examples are the graphs

Gr​(π)=(π+idT∗​M)​(T∗​M),\displaystyle\mathrm{Gr}(\pi)=(\pi+\mathrm{id}_{T^{*}M})(T^{*}M), Gr​(F)=F⊕F∘,\displaystyle\mathrm{Gr}(F)=F\oplus F^{\circ}, Gr​(ω)=(id+ω)​(T​M)\displaystyle\mathrm{Gr}(\omega)=(\mathrm{id}+\omega)(TM)

of a Poisson structure π∈Γ​(∧2T​M)\pi\in\Gamma(\wedge^{2}TM), a foliation F⊂T​MF\subset TM and a closed two-form ω∈Γ​(∧2T∗​M)\omega\in\Gamma(\wedge^{2}T^{*}M). Under favorable circumstances, a Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM may be pulled back by a smooth map into MM, or pushed forward by a map from MM, and condition (2)

i!​Gr​(πM)=p!​Gr​(πY)\displaystyle i^{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})=p^{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{Y})

just says that the pullbacks of πM\pi_{M} and πY\pi_{Y} to XX coincide as Dirac structures.

This paper revolves around a few basic questions. The first concerns characterizing when a Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM can be transported by a diagram

M⟵iX⟶pY\displaystyle M\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i}}{{\longleftarrow}}X\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}Y

where ii is an injective immersion and pp is a smooth surjective submersion, to a Dirac structure on YY:

Theorem (Dirac reduction).

If pp has connected fibres, p!​i!​(LM)p_{!}i^{!}(L_{M}) is a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY exactly when LM​[I]L_{M}[I] is Dirac along ii, where

LM​[I]=LM∩I⟂+I,\displaystyle L_{M}[I]=L_{M}\cap I^{\perp}+I, I:=F⊕N∗​X,\displaystyle I:=F\oplus N^{*}X, F=ker⁡(p∗).\displaystyle F=\ker(p_{*}).

When the conclusions of the above theorem are satisfied, we say that LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction to LYL_{Y} on YY. In the extreme case where X=MX=M and i=idMi=\mathrm{id}_{M}, Dirac reduction becomes the Dirac version of Libermann’s theorem [3]. In the other extreme, when X=YX=Y and p=idXp=\mathrm{id}_{X}, it refines a known criterion for the pullback Dirac structure i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) to be defined.

To state the second basic question we address, let us recall that there is a natural compatibility condition between two Poisson structures, which demands that their sum be again a Poisson structure. This compatibility condition can be expressed for general Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} on MM by demanding that their cotangent product

LM⊛RM={uL+uR+ξ|uL+ξ∈LM,uR+ξ∈RM}\displaystyle L_{M}\circledast R_{M}=\{u_{L}+u_{R}+\xi\ |\ u_{L}+\xi\in L_{M},\ u_{R}+\xi\in R_{M}\}

be again a Dirac structure, in which case we say that LML_{M} and RMR_{M} concur. This notion was systematically developed in [32], where it is argued to be the natural compatibility relation between Dirac structures, and where its main properties are established.

Regrettably, Dirac reduction does not respect concurrence, in the sense that it is perfectly possible that the Dirac reductions to YY do not concur, even if the original Dirac structures on MM concur. However, a reduction scheme of a different nature had been proposed by Marsden and Ra t , iu in [4] in the context of Poisson manifolds (M,πM)(M,\pi_{M}). It equips the submanifold XX with a vector subbundle E⊂T​M|XE\subset TM|_{X} for which F=E∩T​XF=E\cap TX, and demands that E∘E^{\circ} be a Lie subalgebroid of (T∗​M,[⋅,⋅]πM)(T^{*}M,[\cdot,\cdot]^{\pi_{M}}). This ensures a Dirac reduction of πM\pi_{M} to a Poisson structure πY\pi_{Y} — we think of EE as witnessing said Dirac reduction. As was later observed by [7, 9, 12], Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction does preserves concurrence, and that inspired us to extend the condition above on the “witness” EE of the Dirac reduction of πM\pi_{M} to YY to one that makes sense for all Dirac structures, which is accomplished by Definition 20. This extension allows us to state the following concurring reduction scheme generalizing that of Marsden-Ra t , iu222One wonders if this is what Liu, Weinstein and Xu had in mind when they commented that “it would be interesting to interpret and extend the general Marsden–Ra t , iu reduction in terms of Dirac geometry” after [11, Lemma 7.3]. :

Theorem (Concurring reduction).

If concurring Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} on MM have Dirac reductions LYL_{Y} and RYR_{Y} on YY, then also LYL_{Y} and RYR_{Y} concur, provided that their Dirac reductions have a common witness.

In the second part of the paper, we turn to the question of whether there exist “recipes” to construct a common witness for concurring Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M}. The prototypical recipe is what we will nickname

Magri’s original recipe [7, 12] Suppose πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R} are two commuting Poisson structures on MM and X⊂MX\subset M is a leaf of πR\pi_{R}. Then (under favorable circumstances) EL:=πL​(ker⁡πR)⊂T​M|XE_{L}:=\pi_{L}(\ker\pi_{R})\subset TM|_{X} is a witness for both πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R}, and the reduced Poisson structures νL\nu_{L} and νR\nu_{R} on YY also commute.

The two main results in this direction are as follows.

Theorem (Reduction by kernels).

Let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} be concurring Dirac structures, and let K​(LM):=L∩T​MK(L_{M}):=L\cap TM be the kernel of LML_{M} and similarly for K​(RM).K(R_{M}). If the diagram on the left

are the leaf spaces of the foliations on the right, then LML_{M} and RMR_{M} push forward to concurring Dirac structures at every vertex, and those on the bottom vertex are commuting Poisson structures.

The last main theorem gives a generalization of Magri’s original recipe to the Dirac context:

Theorem (Magri’s recipe).

Let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} be concurring Dirac structures. Denote by ELE_{L} and by ERE_{R} the kernel of the Dirac structures

LM⋆(RM⊛ℛ−1​(LM)),\displaystyle L_{M}\star\left(R_{M}\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(L_{M})\right), RM⋆(LM⊛ℛ−1​(RM)),\displaystyle R_{M}\star\left(L_{M}\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(R_{M})\right),

and suppose that maps in the diagram on the left

are the leaf spaces of the foliations on the right. Then LML_{M} and RMR_{M} push forward to concurring Dirac structures at every vertex, and those on the bottom vertex are commuting Poisson structures.

We turn next to illustrating our results in a few settings: the Hamiltonian actions and moment maps of [22], which were already the model example in [2] and [4]; the Dirac-Nijenhuis manifolds of [28], whose Poisson–Nijenhuis ancestors had been treated, in the context of reduction, in [10]; and the case of complex Dirac structures, building on [16, 31], which suggests a novel way to generalize Magri’s recipe. We conclude the paper with with a short discussion contextualizing our contribution within the existing literature.

Acknowledgements

We would like to thank David Martínez Torres, Rui Loja Fernandes, Olivier Brahic, Joel Villatoro, Ping Xu, Marco Zambon and Ioan Mărcu t , for useful conversations and feedback while this paper was being written.

A. Arsie and I. Mencattini are happy to acknowledge the kind support provided by FAPESP, grant number 2025/07987−2.2025/07987-2.

2. Preliminaries

We collect in this section the main definitions we employ in the text. For an introduction to Dirac geometry, see e.g. [6], [21] or [27].

2.1. Dirac structures

Call a subset L⊂𝕋​ML\subset\mathbb{T}M a Lagrangian family if LL meets each fibre 𝕋x​M\mathbb{T}_{x}M in a Lagrangian vector subspace. Collect all such Lagrangian families LL into a set Lag​(𝕋​M)\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M). A smooth map ϕ:M→P\phi:M\to P gives rise to a relation between 𝕋​M\mathbb{T}M and 𝕋​P\mathbb{T}P, where

uM+ξM∼ϕuP+ξP\displaystyle u_{M}+\xi_{M}\sim_{\phi}u_{P}+\xi_{P} ⟺\displaystyle\Longleftrightarrow {ϕ∗​(uM)=uP,ξM=ϕ∗​(ξP).\displaystyle\begin{cases}\phi_{*}(u_{M})=u_{P},\\ \xi_{M}=\phi^{*}(\xi_{P}).\end{cases}

Given Lagrangian families LM∈Lag​(𝕋​M)L_{M}\in\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M) and LP∈Lag​(𝕋​P)L_{P}\in\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}P), we say that

ϕ:(M,LM)→(P,LP)\displaystyle\phi:(M,L_{M})\to(P,L_{P})
is backward:

if, for every x∈Mx\in M and aM∈LM,xa_{M}\in L_{M,x}, there exists aP∈LP,ϕ​(x)a_{P}\in L_{P,\phi(x)} such that aM∼ϕaPa_{M}\sim_{\phi}a_{P};

is forward:

if, for every x∈Mx\in M and aP∈LP,ϕ​(x)a_{P}\in L_{P,\phi(x)}, there exists aM∈LM,xa_{M}\in L_{M,x} such that aM∼ϕaPa_{M}\sim_{\phi}a_{P},

in which cases we write, respectively,

LM=ϕ!​(LP)\displaystyle L_{M}=\phi^{!}(L_{P}) ={aM∈𝕋​M|exists​aP∈LP,aM∼ϕaP},\displaystyle=\{a_{M}\in\mathbb{T}M\ |\ \text{exists}\ a_{P}\in L_{P},\ a_{M}\sim_{\phi}a_{P}\},
{aP∈𝕋​P|exists​aM∈LM,aM∼ϕaP}\displaystyle\{a_{P}\in\mathbb{T}P\ |\ \text{exists}\ a_{M}\in L_{M},\ a_{M}\sim_{\phi}a_{P}\} =ϕ!​(LM)=LP.\displaystyle=\phi_{!}(L_{M})=L_{P}.

If LML_{M} is a Lagrangian family on MM, we write

Γ​(LM)={a∈Γ​(𝕋​M)|a​(M)⊂LM}\displaystyle\Gamma(L_{M})=\{a\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M)\ |\ a(M)\subset L_{M}\}

for the set of smooth sections of 𝕋​M\mathbb{T}M which take values in LML_{M}. When LML_{M} is smooth, i.e. a vector subbundle of 𝕋​M\mathbb{T}M, Γ​(LM)\Gamma(L_{M}) coincides with smooth sections of the vector bundle LML_{M}. A smooth Lagrangian family is a Dirac structure if its space of sections Γ​(LM)\Gamma(L_{M}) is closed under the Dorfman bracket (3). Because LML_{M} is maximally isotropic, a smooth Lagrangian family is Dirac exactly when its Courant tensor

ΥLM∈Γ​(∧3LM∗),\displaystyle\Upsilon_{L_{M}}\in\Gamma(\wedge^{3}L_{M}^{*}), ΥLM​(a,b,c):=⟨[a,b],c⟩\displaystyle\Upsilon_{L_{M}}(a,b,c):=\langle[a,b],c\rangle

vanishes identically.

2.2. Dirac products

We have well-defined tangent- and cotangent products

⋆,⊛:Lag(𝕋M)→Lag(𝕋M),\displaystyle\star,\circledast:\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M)\to\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M),

that is, commutative and associative operations, explicitly given by

L⋆R\displaystyle L\star R ={a+prT∗​(b)|(a,b)∈L×R,prT​(a−b)=0}\displaystyle=\{a+\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(b)\ |\ (a,b)\in L\times R,\ \mathrm{pr}_{T}(a-b)=0\}
L⊛R\displaystyle L\circledast R ={a+prT​(b)|(a,b)∈L×R,prT∗​(a−b)=0}.\displaystyle=\{a+\mathrm{pr}_{T}(b)\ |\ (a,b)\in L\times R,\ \mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(a-b)=0\}.

As explained in [32], the tangent product L⋆RL\star R of two Dirac structures is Dirac, provided that it be smooth. When that happens, leaves of LL and leaves of RR meet cleanly, and the leaves of L⋆RL\star R are the connected components of the intersections of leaves of LL and leaves of RR. We note that L⋆R=ℛω​(L)L\star R=\mathcal{R}_{\omega}(L) if RR is the graph of a two-form ω\omega, and that tangent products are compatible with backward maps, in the sense that, for a smooth map ϕ:N→M\phi:N\to M, we have an equality of Lagrangian families

ϕ!​(L⋆R)=ϕ!​(L)⋆ϕ!​(R).\displaystyle\phi^{!}(L\star R)=\phi^{!}(L)\star\phi^{!}(R).

The cotangent product L⊛RL\circledast R of two Dirac structures need not be Dirac even if L⊛RL\circledast R is smooth. For example, when L=Gr​(πL)L=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{L}) and R=Gr​(πR)R=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{R}) correspond to Poisson structures πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R}, L⊛R=Gr​(πL+πR)L\circledast R=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{L}+\pi_{R}) is Dirac exactly when πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R} commute. More generally, two Dirac structures LL and RR are said to concur if L⊛RL\circledast R is again a Dirac structure. In [32] it is argued that concurrence is the natural notion of “compatibility” between two Dirac structures, and we abide here by that point of view. A fact that will play a key role in the sequel is that the cotangent product is compatible with forward maps, in that

ϕ!​(L⊛R)=ϕ!​(L)⊛ϕ!​(R)\displaystyle\phi_{!}(L\circledast R)=\phi_{!}(L)\circledast\phi_{!}(R)

for any two Lagrangian families LL and RR.

The following language (and notation) from [19] will be convenient in what follows. Let I⊂𝕋​MI\subset\mathbb{T}M be an isotropic family. There is an induced map:

(4) Lag​(𝕋​M)→Lag​(𝕋​M),\displaystyle\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M)\to\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M), L​[I]:=L∩I⟂+I.\displaystyle L[I]:=L\cap I^{\perp}+I.

Note that:

  • •

    when I⊂T∗​MI\subset T^{*}M, we have that

    L​[I]=L⋆Gr​(I);\displaystyle L[I]=L\star\mathrm{Gr}(I);
  • •

    when I⊂T​MI\subset TM, we have that

    L​[I]=L⊛Gr​(I).\displaystyle L[I]=L\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(I).

Note also that, for a Dirac structure LL, a sufficient condition for L​[I]L[I] to be smooth is that L∩IL\cap I be smooth.

2.3. Involutivity along an injective immersion

We introduce the notion of involutivity along an injectively immersed submanifold. This is a localization along XX of the usual notion of involutivity along an embedded submanifold.

Definition 1.

Let i:X→Mi:X\to M be an injective immersion, and D⊂𝕋​M|XD\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{X} a vector subbundle. We say that DD is involutive along ii if

Γ​(𝕋​M,D|V)={s∈Γ​(𝕋​M)|s|V∈Γ​(D|V)}\displaystyle\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M,D|_{V})=\{s\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M)\ |\ s|_{V}\in\Gamma(D|_{V})\}

is a subalgebra of Γ​(𝕋​M)\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M) for every connected, open set V⊂XV\subset X on which ii restricts to an embedding i:V→Mi:V\to M — that is, [a,b]∈Γ​(𝕋​M,D|V)[a,b]\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M,D|_{V}) whenever a,b∈Γ​(𝕋​M,D|V)a,b\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M,D|_{V}).

Example 2.

For any submanifold X⊂MX\subset M, N∗​X⟂=T​X⊕T∗​M|XN^{*}X^{\perp}=TX\oplus T^{*}M|_{X} is involutive. Equivalently, if the anchors of a1,a2∈Γ​(𝕋​M)a_{1},a_{2}\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M) are tangent to XX, so is their Dorfman bracket [a1,a2][a_{1},a_{2}].

Example 3.

Let i:X→Mi:X\to M be an injective immersion, and let LXL_{X} be a Dirac structure on XX. Then

i!​(LX)⊂𝕋​M|X,\displaystyle i_{!}(L_{X})\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{X}, i!​(LX)={u+ξ|u+i∗​(ξ)∈LX}\displaystyle i_{!}(L_{X})=\{u+\xi\ |\ u+i^{*}(\xi)\in L_{X}\}

is a Dirac structure along ii.

Lemma 4.

Let i:X→Mi:X\to M be an injective immersion. An isotropic subbundle D⊂𝕋​M|XD\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{X} for which prT​(D)⊂T​X\mathrm{pr}_{T}(D)\subset TX inherits a Lie algebroid structure if it is involutive along ii.

Proof.

Case 1: ii is an embedding. By the Leibniz property, [a1,a2]|X=0[a_{1},a_{2}]|_{X}=0 if a2|X=0a_{2}|_{X}=0. Indeed, it suffices to argue on a neighborhood in MM of each point of XX. There we have a frame ζ1,…,ζ2​m\zeta_{1},...,\zeta_{2m} of 𝕋​M\mathbb{T}M, so

[a1,a2]=∑i=12​m[a1,fi​ζi]=∑i=12​mfi​[a1,ζi]+∑i=12​m⟨d​fi,prT​(a1)⟩​ζi.\displaystyle[a_{1},a_{2}]=\sum_{i=1}^{2m}[a_{1},f_{i}\zeta_{i}]=\sum_{i=1}^{2m}f_{i}[a_{1},\zeta_{i}]+\sum_{i=1}^{2m}\langle\mathrm{d}f_{i},\mathrm{pr}_{T}(a_{1})\rangle\zeta_{i}.

Because prT​(a1)\mathrm{pr}_{T}(a_{1}) is tangent to XX, it follows that, if fi|X=0f_{i}|_{X}=0 for all i=1,…,2​mi=1,...,2m, then [a1,a2]|X=0[a_{1},a_{2}]|_{X}=0. This implies that

[a1|X,a2|X]:=[a1,a2]|X\displaystyle[a_{1}|_{X},a_{2}|_{X}]:=[a_{1},a_{2}]|_{X}

gives a well-defined Lie algebra structure on Γ​(D)\Gamma(D), and the induced bracket of DD can be computed using arbitrary extensions.

Case 2: ii is an injective immersion. Because the condition of DD being a Lie subalgebroid is local in XX, it suffices to show that D|V⊂𝕋​M|VD|_{V}\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{V} is a Lie subalgebroid for an open neighborhood VV of every point x∈Xx\in X. Choose a connected V⊂XV\subset X for which i:V→Mi:V\to M is an embedding, and observe that the condition of involutivity along ii on DD implies that we are in the setting of Case 1, and therefore D|VD|_{V} is a Lie subalgebroid. ∎

Definition 5.

A Lagrangian subbundle D⊂𝕋​M|XD\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{X} is called a Dirac structure along ii if prT​(D)⊂T​X\mathrm{pr}_{T}(D)\subset TX and DD is involutive along ii.

Remark 6.

When XX is an embedded submanifold, the condition of involutivity along ii in Definition 1 reduces to “involutivity along XX”, that is, Γ​(𝕋​M,D)\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M,D) being a subalgebra. When XX is not embedded, involutivity along ii is a finer notion, as the example below illustrates.

Example 7.

Let M=ℝ5M=\mathbb{R}^{5} and let X=ℝ4×ℚX=\mathbb{R}^{4}\times\mathbb{Q}. Define nowhere-vanishing sections w1,w2∈Γ​(T​M|X)w_{1},w_{2}\in\Gamma(TM|_{X}) by

w1​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)=∂∂x3+x2​∂∂x4,\displaystyle w_{1}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right)=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}+x_{2}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}, w2​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)=∂∂x1+q​∂∂x2,\displaystyle w_{2}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right)=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}+q\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}},

where pq∈ℚ\tfrac{p}{q}\in\mathbb{Q} is in reduced form333That is, pp and q>0q>0 have no common prime divisor if p≠0p\neq 0, and q=1q=1 if p=0p=0., and subbundles F,E⊂T​M|XF,E\subset TM|_{X} by

F=⟨w1⟩,\displaystyle F=\langle w_{1}\rangle, E=⟨w1,w2⟩.\displaystyle E=\langle w_{1},w_{2}\rangle.

Observe that a vector field v∈Γ​(T​M)v\in\Gamma(TM), v=∑i=15vi​∂∂xiv=\sum_{i=1}^{5}v_{i}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{i}} lies in EE at (x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right) iff

v2​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)\displaystyle v_{2}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right) =q​v1​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq),\displaystyle=qv_{1}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right),
v4​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)\displaystyle v_{4}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right) =x2​v3​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq).\displaystyle=x_{2}v_{3}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right).

Fix a reduced fraction pq∈ℚ\tfrac{p}{q}\in\mathbb{Q} and observe that the sequence

1+n​p​qn​q2,\displaystyle\tfrac{1+npq}{nq^{2}}, n∈ℕ\displaystyle n\in\mathbb{N}

is made of reduced fractions only, and

pq=limn→∞1+n​p​qn​q2.\displaystyle\tfrac{p}{q}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\tfrac{1+npq}{nq^{2}}.

Therefore

v2​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)\displaystyle v_{2}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right) =limn→∞v2​(x1,x2,x3,x4,1+n​p​qn​q2)\displaystyle=\lim_{n\to\infty}v_{2}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{1+npq}{nq^{2}}\right)
=limn→∞n​q2​v1​(x1,x2,x3,x4,1+n​p​qn​q2)\displaystyle=\lim_{n\to\infty}nq^{2}v_{1}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{1+npq}{nq^{2}}\right)

implies that

v1​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)=limn→∞v1​(x1,x2,x3,x4,1+n​p​qn​q2)=0,\displaystyle v_{1}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right)=\lim_{n\to\infty}v_{1}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{1+npq}{nq^{2}}\right)=0,

and therefore

v2​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)=q​v1​(x1,x2,x3,x4,pq)=0.\displaystyle v_{2}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right)=qv_{1}\left(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},\tfrac{p}{q}\right)=0.

This implies that Γ​(T​M,E)=Γ​(T​M,F)\Gamma(TM,E)=\Gamma(TM,F) is a Lie subalgebra. However, for each connected component Y⊂XY\subset X, we have that Γ​(T​M,E|Y)≠Γ​(T​M,F|Y)\Gamma(TM,E|_{Y})\neq\Gamma(TM,F|_{Y}), and Γ​(T​M,E|Y)\Gamma(TM,E|_{Y}) is not a Lie subalgebra, since

[∂∂x1+q​∂∂x2,∂∂x3+x2​∂∂x4]=q​∂∂x4∉Γ​(T​M,E|Y).\displaystyle\left[\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}+q\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}+x_{2}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}\right]=q\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}\not\in\Gamma(TM,E|_{Y}).

Said otherwise, Gr​(E)⊂𝕋​M\mathrm{Gr}(E)\subset\mathbb{T}M is not a Dirac structure along ii, even though Γ​(T​M,E)\Gamma(TM,E) is a Lie subalgebra.

Observe that the operations of Dirac products and of closest Lagrangians (4) have obvious extensions to the “along XX” setting. For example,

L​[I]=L⋆Gr​(T​X),\displaystyle L[I]=L\star\mathrm{Gr}(TX), if I=N∗​X,\displaystyle I=N^{*}X,
L​[I]=L⊛Gr​(I),\displaystyle L[I]=L\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(I), if I⊂T​M|X.\displaystyle I\subset TM|_{X}.

3. Induced and projectable Dirac structures

In this section, we discuss the operations of inducing a Dirac structure by an injective immersion i:X→(M,LM)i:X\to(M,L_{M}), and pushing forward a Dirac structure through a surjective submersion p:(X,LX)→Yp:(X,L_{X})\to Y. These operations will find a common generalization (“Dirac reduction”) in the next section.

One of the crucial features of the Dirac formalism is that there is a canonical candidate for a Dirac structure induced on a submanifold by a Dirac structure on the ambient manifold. Here we characterize when that candidate is actually Dirac, emphasizing necessary and sufficient conditions read off from the ambient Dirac structure along the submanifold.

Lemma 8.

For an injective immersion i:X→Mi:X\to M into a Dirac manifold (M,LM)(M,L_{M}), the following are equivalent:

  1. i)

    LM​[N∗​X]L_{M}[N^{*}X] is a vector subbundle of 𝕋​M|X\mathbb{T}M|_{X};

  2. ii)

    LM​[N∗​X]L_{M}[N^{*}X] is a Dirac structure along ii;

  3. iii)

    XX has an induced Dirac structure i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}).

Proof.

As in the proof of Lemma 4, all three conditions in the statement hold exactly when they hold for every connected open set V⊂XV\subset X that ii embeds into MM, so we may assume in the course of the proof below that XX is embedded.

i) implies ii) LM​[N∗​X]=LM∩N∗​X⟂+N∗​XL_{M}[N^{*}X]=L_{M}\cap N^{*}X^{\perp}+N^{*}X is involutive, since

a,b∈Γ​(𝕋​M),\displaystyle a,b\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M), a|X∈Γ​(N∗​X⟂),\displaystyle a|_{X}\in\Gamma(N^{*}X^{\perp}), b|X∈Γ​(N∗​X)\displaystyle b|_{X}\in\Gamma(N^{*}X) ⇒\displaystyle\Rightarrow [a,b]|X∈Γ​(N∗​X)\displaystyle[a,b]|_{X}\in\Gamma(N^{*}X)

and N∗​X⟂N^{*}X^{\perp} is involutive.

ii) implies iii) If LM​[N∗​X]L_{M}[N^{*}X] is Dirac, then

i!​(LM​[N∗​X])=i!​(LM)\displaystyle i^{!}(L_{M}[N^{*}X])=i^{!}(L_{M})

is smooth, since the canonical map

LM​[N∗​X]→i!​(LM),\displaystyle L_{M}[N^{*}X]\to i^{!}(L_{M}), u+ξ↦u+i∗​(ξ)\displaystyle u+\xi\mapsto u+i^{*}(\xi)

is surjective. As a smooth pullback of a Dirac structure it follows that i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) is Dirac.

iii) implies i) If i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) is Dirac, then

i!​(i!​(LM))\displaystyle i_{!}(i^{!}(L_{M})) ={i∗​(v)+η+ζ∈𝕋​M|v+η∈LM,ζ∈N∗​X}\displaystyle=\{i_{*}(v)+\eta+\zeta\in\mathbb{T}M\ |\ v+\eta\in L_{M},\ \zeta\in N^{*}X\}
=LM∩N∗​X⟂+N∗​X\displaystyle=L_{M}\cap N^{*}X^{\perp}+N^{*}X
=LM​[N∗​X]\displaystyle=L_{M}[N^{*}X]

is a smooth subbundle (as is the forward image of any Lagrangian subbundle of 𝕋​X\mathbb{T}X). ∎

Remark 9.

The Dirac structure i!​(L)i^{!}(L) induced on an embedded submanifold i:X→Mi:X\to M by a Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM may bear little resemblance to the ambient Dirac structure. The key issue is that a leaf of the induced Dirac structure need not lie inside a leaf of the ambient Dirac structure. These pathologies are discussed in [30, Section 2], from which we extract the following illustrative example:

Example 10.

Let M=ℝ4M=\mathbb{R}^{4} be equipped with the Dirac structure LML_{M} which is the graph of the Poisson structure

πM=∂∂x1∧∂∂x2+x3​∂∂x3∧∂∂x4.\displaystyle\pi_{M}=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+x_{3}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}.

Let f∈C∞​(ℝ2)f\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{R}^{2}) be any smooth function, and consider the embedding

i:ℝ2→M,\displaystyle i:\mathbb{R}^{2}\to M, i​(x1,x2)=(x1,x2,f​(x1,x2)2,f​(x1,x2)2).\displaystyle i(x_{1},x_{2})=(x_{1},x_{2},f(x_{1},x_{2})^{2},f(x_{1},x_{2})^{2}).

Because

i!​(LM)=Gr​(∂∂x1∧∂∂x2),\displaystyle i^{!}(L_{M})=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}\right),

the submanifold X:=i​(ℝ2)X:=i(\mathbb{R}^{2}) has an induced symplectic structure. However, if f−1​(0)f^{-1}(0) is nonempty, XX hits more than one leaf of LML_{M}.

A wide class of induced Dirac structures for which no such pathologies arise is that of split induced structures — the ones for which sections of the induced structure come from sections of the ambient structure:

Definition 11.

An injective immersion i:X→Mi:X\to M splits a Dirac structure LM⊂𝕋​ML_{M}\subset\mathbb{T}M if i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) is Dirac on XX, and every section aX∈Γ​(i!​(L))a_{X}\in\Gamma(i^{!}(L)) is ii-related to a section of LML_{M}.

Lemma 12 (Split condition).

For an embedded submanifold X⊂MX\subset M and a Lagrangian subbundle LM⊂𝕋​ML_{M}\subset\mathbb{T}M, the following are equivalent:

  1. i)

    a vector subbundle D⊂LMD\subset L_{M} exists, such that LM​[N∗​X]=D⊕N∗​XL_{M}[N^{*}X]=D\oplus N^{*}X;

  2. ii)

    i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) is smooth, and every one of its sections is related to a section of LML_{M}.

Proof.

If LM​[N∗​X]=D⊕N∗​XL_{M}[N^{*}X]=D\oplus N^{*}X for some subbundle D⊂LM|XD\subset L_{M}|_{X}, then i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) is smooth by Lemma 8. Moreover, every section aXa_{X} of i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) can be lifted to a section aDa_{D} of DD. Because D⊂LM|XD\subset L_{M}|_{X}, the section aDa_{D} of DD has a local extension to a section aMa_{M} of LML_{M} in a neighborhood of XX. Therefore aM∼aXa_{M}\sim a_{X}, and so i) implies ii).

Conversely, suppose sections of i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) are related to sections of LML_{M}. We construct a linear splitting σ:i!​(L)→N∗​X\sigma:i^{!}(L)\to N^{*}X to the canonical map i!:L​[N∗​X]→i!​(L)i^{!}:L[N^{*}X]\to i^{!}(L), with the property that

(5) im​(σ)⊂N∗​X⟂.\displaystyle\mathrm{im}(\sigma)\subset N^{*}X^{\perp}.

Because condition (5) is convex, a linear map σ\sigma as above can be constructed from linear maps

σi:i!​(LM)|Ui→N∗​Ui,\displaystyle\sigma_{i}:i^{!}(L_{M})|_{U_{i}}\to N^{*}U_{i}, σisatisfies​(5)\displaystyle\sigma_{i}\ \ \text{satisfies}\ \eqref{eq : convex condition}

defined on the open sets of an open cover 𝒰=(Ui)\mathscr{U}=(U_{i}) of XX, and a partition of unity (ρi)(\rho_{i}) subordinated to 𝒰\mathscr{U}:

σ=∑iρi​σi.\displaystyle\sigma=\sum_{i}\rho_{i}\sigma_{i}.

It suffices then to argue the case where i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) has a global frame a1,…,an∈Γ​(i!​(LM))a_{1},...,a_{n}\in\Gamma(i^{!}(L_{M})), in which case σ\sigma can be uniquely defined by requiring that σ​(ai)=a~i\sigma(a_{i})=\widetilde{a}_{i} for any a~1,…,a~n∈Γ​(LM)\widetilde{a}_{1},...,\widetilde{a}_{n}\in\Gamma(L_{M}) with a~i∼ai\widetilde{a}_{i}\sim a_{i}. ∎

We recall next the Dirac version of Libermann’s theorem, which provides necessary and sufficient conditions for a Dirac structure to be pushed forward to a Dirac structure by a surjective submersion with connected fibres. The question is: given a surjective submersion with connected fibres p:X→Yp:X\to Y, when does a Dirac structure LXL_{X} on XX push forward under pp to a Dirac structure on YY ? That is: when is there a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY, for which

p:(X,LX)→(Y,LY)\displaystyle p:(X,L_{X})\to(Y,L_{Y})

is a forward Dirac map?

Lemma 13 (Libermann’s theorem).

Let p:X→Yp:X\to Y be a surjective submersion with connected fibres, and LXL_{X} a Dirac structure on XX. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. i)

    LXL_{X} pushes forward under pp to a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY;

  2. ii)

    LX​[F]=LX⊛Gr​(F)L_{X}[F]=L_{X}\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(F) is Dirac, where F⊂T​XF\subset TX denotes the pp-vertical foliation,

in which case

p!​(LY)=LX​[F].\displaystyle p^{!}(L_{Y})=L_{X}[F].
Proof.

If p!​(LX)=LYp_{!}(L_{X})=L_{Y}, then LX​[F]=p!​p!​(LX)=p!​(LY)L_{X}[F]=p^{!}p_{!}(L_{X})=p^{!}(L_{Y}) is Dirac. Conversely, because F⊂LX​[F]F\subset L_{X}[F], if LX​[F]L_{X}[F] is Dirac, then

p!​(LX,x)=p!​(LX​[F]x)=p!​(LX​[F]x′)=p!​(LX,x′)\displaystyle p_{!}(L_{X,x})=p_{!}(L_{X}[F]_{x})=p_{!}(L_{X}[F]_{x^{\prime}})=p_{!}(L_{X,x^{\prime}})

for any x,x′∈Xx,x^{\prime}\in X in the same FF-leaf (see [26, Proposition 1] for details). Thus p!​(LX)=p!​(LX​[F])p_{!}(L_{X})=p_{!}(L_{X}[F]) defines a Lagrangian subbundle LYL_{Y} on YY, which is Dirac because any two sections aY,bY∈Γ​(LY)a_{Y},b_{Y}\in\Gamma(L_{Y}) are pp-related to sections aX,bX∈Γ​(LX​[F])a_{X},b_{X}\in\Gamma(L_{X}[F]), and therefore

[aX,bX]∈Γ​(LX​[F]),\displaystyle[a_{X},b_{X}]\in\Gamma(L_{X}[F]), [aX,bX]∼[aY,bY]\displaystyle[a_{X},b_{X}]\sim[a_{Y},b_{Y}] ⟹\displaystyle\Longrightarrow [aY,bY]∈Γ​(LY).\displaystyle[a_{Y},b_{Y}]\in\Gamma(L_{Y}).

Hence ii) implies i). ∎

4. Dirac reduction

We characterize the minimal reduction scheme, which we call “Dirac reduction”, and observe it to be incompatible with concurrence.

The two basic operations of pulling back a Dirac structure by an injective immersion, and of pushing forward a Dirac structure under a surjective submersion have a common generalization in the notion of Dirac reduction, which we discuss next.

We fix a diagram (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) M⟵iX⟶pY\displaystyle M\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i}}{{\longleftarrow}}X\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}Y where: • i:X→Mi:X\to M is an injective immersion; • p:X→Yp:X\to Y is a surjective submersion with connected fibres. Denote by F⊂T​X,\displaystyle F\subset TX, F:=ker⁡(p∗:T​X→T​Y)\displaystyle F:=\ker(p_{*}:TX\to TY) the vertical bundle to pp, and by II the isotropic subbundle I⊂𝕋​M|X,\displaystyle I\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{X}, I:=F⊕N∗​X.\displaystyle I:=F\oplus N^{*}X. We say that LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction to YY if there is a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY such that p!​i!​(LM)=LY.\displaystyle p_{!}i^{!}(L_{M})=L_{Y}.
Theorem 14 (Dirac reduction).

Given a diagram (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) as above, the following conditions on a Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM are equivalent:

  1. i)

    LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction to YY;

  2. ii)

    i!​(LM)​[F]i^{!}(L_{M})[F] is Dirac on XX;

  3. iii)

    LM​[I]L_{M}[I] is Dirac along ii.

Proof.

If a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY exists, such that

LY=p!​i!​(LM),\displaystyle L_{Y}=p_{!}i^{!}(L_{M}),

then

p!​(LY)=p!​p!​i!​(LM)=i!​(LM)​[F]\displaystyle p^{!}(L_{Y})=p^{!}p_{!}i^{!}(L_{M})=i^{!}(L_{M})[F]

by Lemma 13. Therefore i) implies ii). Now, because

i!​(LM)​[F]=i!​(LM​[F])=i!​(LM​[F]​[N∗​X])=i!​(LM​[I])\displaystyle i^{!}(L_{M})[F]=i^{!}(L_{M}[F])=i^{!}(L_{M}[F][N^{*}X])=i^{!}(L_{M}[I])

we see that as in Example 3 that

i!​(i!​(LM)​[F])=i!​i!​(LM​[I])=LM​[I]\displaystyle i_{!}\left(i^{!}(L_{M})[F]\right)=i_{!}i^{!}(L_{M}[I])=L_{M}[I]

is Dirac along ii. Thus ii) implies iii). Finally, if LM​[I]=LM​[F]​[N∗​X]L_{M}[I]=L_{M}[F][N^{*}X] is Dirac along ii, then by Lemma 8,

i!​(LM​[F]​[N∗​X])=i!​(LM​[F])=i!​(LM)​[F]\displaystyle i^{!}(L_{M}[F][N^{*}X])=i^{!}(L_{M}[F])=i^{!}(L_{M})[F]

is Dirac on XX, so again by Lemma 13, a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY exists, such that

p!​(LY)=i!​(LM)​[F]=p!​p!​i!​(LM),\displaystyle p^{!}(L_{Y})=i^{!}(L_{M})[F]=p^{!}p_{!}i^{!}(L_{M}),

and therefore

LY=p!​i!​(LM),\displaystyle L_{Y}=p_{!}i^{!}(L_{M}),

so iii) implies i). ∎

Example 15.

We highlight that Dirac reduction under (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) does not assume (or imply) that XX itself inherits a Dirac structure from LML_{M}. For example, consider the triangle

ℝ2⟵iX⟶pY={∗}\displaystyle\mathbb{R}^{2}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i}}{{\longleftarrow}}X\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}Y=\{\ast\}

where X={x∈M|x2=0}X=\{x\in M\ |\ x_{2}=0\}. Then any Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM Dirac reduces to the only Dirac structure on the one-point space YY. However, XX need not inherit a Dirac structure: for example, if

LM=Gr​(πM),\displaystyle L_{M}=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M}), πM=x1​∂∂x1∧∂∂x2,\displaystyle\pi_{M}=x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}},

then

i!​(LM)x={Tx​X,if​x≠0;Tx∗​Xif​x=0\displaystyle i^{!}(L_{M})_{x}=\begin{cases}T_{x}X,&\text{if}\ x\neq 0;\\ T^{*}_{x}X&\text{if}\ x=0\end{cases}

and therefore i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) is not smooth.

In this paper, we espouse the point of view that “concurrence” is the canonical compatibility relation between Dirac structures. A natural question that arises at this point is:

Does the Dirac reduction scheme of Theorem 14 respect concurrence? Namely, if two Dirac structures on MM concur weakly, and have Dirac reductions to YY, do the reduced Dirac structures on YY concur weakly?

That turns out not to be the case, as the following example illustrates:

Example 16 (Dirac reduction does not respect concurrence).

Consider on M=ℝ4M=\mathbb{R}^{4} the commuting Poisson structures

πL=∂∂x1∧∂∂x4,\displaystyle\pi_{L}=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}, πR=(∂∂x2+x1​∂∂x3)∧∂∂x4.\displaystyle\pi_{R}=\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\right)\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}.

Let X⊂MX\subset M be the submanifold given by x4=0x_{4}=0. Then

i!​Gr​(πL)=Gr​(∂∂x1),\displaystyle i^{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{L})=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}), i!​Gr​(πR)=Gr​(∂∂x2+x1​∂∂x3)\displaystyle i^{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{R})=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}})

do not concur:

⟨[∂∂x1,∂∂x2+x1​∂∂x3],d​x3−x1​d​x2⟩=1.\displaystyle\langle[\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}],\mathrm{d}x_{3}-x_{1}\mathrm{d}x_{2}\rangle=1.

In the sequel, we will address this lack of compatibility of Dirac reduction with concurrence by devising a more selective reduction scheme under which concurrence is compatible. As in the classical reduction scheme proposed by Marsden and Ra t , iu in the context of Poisson structures, the generalization we propose to Dirac structures requires that the Dirac reduction be witnessed by a certain subbundle E⊂T​M|XE\subset TM|_{X}.

5. Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction

We recall the Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction scheme for Poisson structures, and highlight its concurring nature that we will later seek to generalize to Dirac structures.

In the context of Poisson geometry, one reduction scheme was proposed by Jerrold Marsden and Tudor Ra t , iu in [4]. It depends on a choice of an adapted subbundle to (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) — that is,

(𝔈\mathfrak{E}) E⊂T​M|X,\displaystyle E\subset TM|_{X}, F=E∩T​X,\displaystyle F=E\cap TX,

which we think of as an extra structure on (𝔗\mathfrak{T}):

Theorem 17 (Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction).

Let (M,πM)(M,\pi_{M}) be a Poisson manifold, and denote by 𝔤\mathfrak{g} the Lie algebra C∞​(M)C^{\infty}(M), equipped with the Poisson bracket of πM\pi_{M}. If ii in (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) is an embedding, and an adapted subbundle (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) exists, such that

  1. MR1)

    πM♯​(E∘)⊂T​X+E\pi_{M}^{\sharp}(E^{\circ})\subset TX+E, and

  2. MR2)

    the subset

    C∞​(M)E⊂𝔤,\displaystyle C^{\infty}(M)_{E}\subset\mathfrak{g}, C∞​(M)E={H∈C∞​(M)|d​H​(E)=0}\displaystyle C^{\infty}(M)_{E}=\{H\in C^{\infty}(M)\ |\ \mathrm{d}H(E)=0\}

    is a Poisson subalgebra,

then πM\pi_{M} Dirac reduces to a Poisson structure πY\pi_{Y} on YY, and we have that

p!​(Gr​(πY))=i!​(Gr​(πM)​[E]).\displaystyle p^{!}(\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{Y}))=i^{!}\left(\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[E]\right).
Remark 18.

It was observed in [17, Lemma 2.2] that, under condition MR2), condition MR1) is equivalent to

  1. MR1’)

    πM♯​(E∘)⊂T​X\pi_{M}^{\sharp}(E^{\circ})\subset TX.

Otherwise said [17, Remark 2.2], conditions MR1) and MR2) are equivalent to

ℛπM​(E∘)={πM♯​(ξ)+ξ|ξ∈E∘}\displaystyle\mathcal{R}_{\pi_{M}}(E^{\circ})=\{\pi_{M}^{\sharp}(\xi)+\xi\ |\ \xi\in E^{\circ}\}

being a Lie subalgebroid of Gr​(πM)\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M}) over XX.

Proof of Theorem 17.

Because

ℛπM​(E∘)⊂ℛπM​(F∘)∩(N∗​X)⟂,\displaystyle\mathcal{R}_{\pi_{M}}(E^{\circ})\subset\mathcal{R}_{\pi_{M}}(F^{\circ})\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp},

by Remark 18, we have that

Gr​(πM)​[E]​[N∗​X]=Gr​(πM)​[F]​[N∗​X]=Gr​(πM)​[I]=F+ℛπ​(E∘)+N∗​X\displaystyle\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[E][N^{*}X]=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[F][N^{*}X]=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[I]=F+\mathcal{R}_{\pi}(E^{\circ})+N^{*}X

Because FF and N∗​XN^{*}X are involutive, and ℛπ​(E∘)\mathcal{R}_{\pi}(E^{\circ}) is involutive by Remark 18, it follows from Theorem 14 that Gr​(πM)\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M}) Dirac reduces to a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY. This is a Poisson structure because

i!​(Gr​(πM))∩T​X=F\displaystyle i^{!}(\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M}))\cap TX=F

and therefore LY∩T​Y=0L_{Y}\cap TY=0. This implies that LYL_{Y} is the graph of a Poisson structure πY\pi_{Y} on YY. ∎

Example 19.

Much like with Dirac reduction, the conditions for Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction do not ensure that XX itself is Dirac. As an illustration, in Example 15, the adapted subbundle E=T​XE=TX satisfies MR1) and MR2).

In the taxonomy we propose here, “Dirac reduction” as formulated in Theorem 14 consists of a minimal reduction scheme — that is, the least stringent one which allows the transfer of a Dirac structure on the ambient manifold to the quotient of a submanifold.

A key fact we wish to highlight in this note is that the Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction is a concurrent reduction scheme — that is, one which respects the compatibility relation of “concurrence”, a fact which seems to have first been observed in [7, Proposition 1.1.].

6. Witnesses of concurrent reduction

In this section, we propose our generalization of the Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction scheme to Dirac structures, and discuss the properties it satisfies.

Given a triangle (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) as before,

M⟵iX⟶pY,\displaystyle M\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i}}{{\longleftarrow}}X\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}Y,

the problem is to propose a reduction scheme that respects concurrence. More precisely, we wish to propose a scheme of reduction for Dirac structures, which meets the following requirements:

CR1) It implies Dirac reduction under (𝔗\mathfrak{T}); CR2) It should boil down to the Marsden-Ratiu reduction when XX is embedded and LML_{M} corresponds to a Poisson structure; CR3) It should boil down to Libermann’s theorem when X=MX=M; CR4) It respects concurrence in the sense that the Dirac reductions under (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) of concurring Dirac structures should also concur (perhaps weakly).

The compatibility condition the current reduction scheme demands is encoded in the following definition:

Definition 20.
A subbundle (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) is a witness for a Lagrangian family LML_{M} on MM if: Wit1) LM​[I]L_{M}[I] is a vector bundle, where I:=F⊕N∗​XI:=F\oplus N^{*}X; Wit2) LM​[E]∩N∗​X⟂L_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}X^{\perp} is involutive along ii; Wit3) LM∩E⟂⊂N∗​X⟂L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}\subset N^{*}X^{\perp}.
Proposition 21 (CR1 holds).

A Dirac structure LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction under (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) if a witness (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) exists for it.

Proof.

Let (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) be a witness for a Dirac structure LML_{M}. By Wit1), LM​[I]L_{M}[I] is a vector bundle; and by Wit3),

(6) LM​[I]=LM​[E]​[N∗​X]=LM​[E]∩N∗​X⟂+N∗​X,\displaystyle L_{M}[I]=L_{M}[E][N^{*}X]=L_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}X^{\perp}+N^{*}X,

which is Dirac along ii by Wit2). Hence by Theorem 14, LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction to a Dirac structure on YY. ∎

Remark 22.

One could also wonder whether it is possible to do away with the extra choice of adapted subbundle (𝔈\mathfrak{E}), and restrict our attention to (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) alone. After all, the reduced Dirac structure only depends on FF, and not on EE. It turns out that demanding that E=FE=F be canonical for a Dirac structure LML_{M} is excessively restrictive. For example,

LM=Gr​(πM),\displaystyle L_{M}=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M}), πM=∂∂x1∧∂∂x2+∂∂x3∧∂∂x4\displaystyle\pi_{M}=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}

is a Dirac structure on M=ℝ4M=\mathbb{R}^{4}. Consider X⊂MX\subset M given by x3=0=x4x_{3}=0=x_{4}, and p:X→Y=ℝp:X\to Y=\mathbb{R}, p​(x1,x2,0,0)=x1p(x_{1},x_{2},0,0)=x_{1}. Then

LM​[I]=⟨d​x1,∂∂x2,d​x3,d​x4⟩,\displaystyle L_{M}[I]=\left\langle\mathrm{d}x_{1},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}},\mathrm{d}x_{3},\mathrm{d}x_{4}\right\rangle,

so LML_{M} reduces to LY=T∗​YL_{Y}=T^{*}Y. Note that

LM∩F⟂=⟨∂∂x2+d​x1,∂∂x4+d​x3,∂∂x3−d​x4⟩\displaystyle L_{M}\cap F^{\perp}=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+\mathrm{d}x_{1},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}+\mathrm{d}x_{3},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}-\mathrm{d}x_{4}\right\rangle

does not satisfy MR1’). However, the vector subbundle

E⊂T​M|X,\displaystyle E\subset TM|_{X}, E=⟨∂∂x2,∂∂x3,∂∂x4⟩\displaystyle E=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}\right\rangle

does satisfy MR1) and MR2).

Proposition 23 (CR2 holds).

When ii in (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) is an embedding, (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) satisfies the MR conditions for πM\pi_{M} exactly when (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) is a witness for Gr​(πM)\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M}), in which case the reduced Dirac structure is again Poisson.

Proof.

Condition MR1’) is equivalent to Wit3), and under it, Wit1) is satisfied, because

Gr​(πM)​[I]\displaystyle\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[I] =(6)Gr​(πM)​[E]​[N∗​X]\displaystyle\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\eqref{eq: wit3 implies}}}{{=}}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[E][N^{*}X]
=(E⊕ℛπ​(E∘))​[N∗​X]\displaystyle=\left(E\oplus\mathcal{R}_{\pi}(E^{\circ})\right)[N^{*}X]
=F⊕ℛπ​(E∘)+N∗​X\displaystyle=F\oplus\mathcal{R}_{\pi}(E^{\circ})+N^{*}X

and the latter is smooth because F=E∩T​XF=E\cap TX is smooth, and therefore so is

(F⊕ℛπ​(E∘))∩N∗​X=E∘∩N∗​X.\displaystyle\left(F\oplus\mathcal{R}_{\pi}(E^{\circ})\right)\cap N^{*}X=E^{\circ}\cap N^{*}X.

It remains to show that MR2) and Wit2) are equivalent conditions. Under either hypothesis, we have that

i!​(Gr​(πM)​[E])=i!​(Gr​(πM))​[F]=p!​(LY)\displaystyle i^{!}(\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[E])=i^{!}(\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M}))[F]=p^{!}(L_{Y})

for a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY. Because

i!​(Gr​(πM)​[E])∩T​X={v+πM​(ξ)|v∈F,ξ∈E∘∩N∗​X}⊂F\displaystyle i^{!}(\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[E])\cap TX=\{v+\pi_{M}(\xi)\ |\ v\in F,\ \xi\in E^{\circ}\cap N^{*}X\}\subset F

since MR1’) implies that πM​(E∘∩N∗​X)⊂F\pi_{M}(E^{\circ}\cap N^{*}X)\subset F, we deduce that the Dirac structure LYL_{Y} meets T​YTY trivially, and is therefore the graph of a Poisson structure πY\pi_{Y} on YY.

Next observe that if ξ,η∈Ω1​(M)\xi,\eta\in\Omega^{1}(M) are such that ξ|X,η|X∈Γ​(E∘)\xi|_{X},\eta|_{X}\in\Gamma(E^{\circ}), then Wit2) implies that there exist v∈Γ​(F)v\in\Gamma(F) and ζ∈Γ​(E∘)\zeta\in\Gamma(E^{\circ}) for which

[πM​(ξ)+ξ,πM​(η)+η]=[πM​(ξ),πM​(η)]+[ξ,η]πM=v+πM​(ζ)+ζ,\displaystyle[\pi_{M}(\xi)+\xi,\pi_{M}(\eta)+\eta]=[\pi_{M}(\xi),\pi_{M}(\eta)]+[\xi,\eta]^{\pi_{M}}=v+\pi_{M}(\zeta)+\zeta,

and this in turn implies that ζ=[ξ,η]πM\zeta=[\xi,\eta]^{\pi_{M}} and v=0v=0. Therefore Wit2) ⇒\Rightarrow MR2). To prove the converse implication, let

𝒮={ξ∈Γ​(E∘)|i∗​(ξ)∈p∗​Ω1​(Y)}.\displaystyle\mathcal{S}=\{\xi\in\Gamma(E^{\circ})\ |\ i^{*}(\xi)\in p^{*}\Omega^{1}(Y)\}.

Assume MR2) and choose ξ∈𝒮\xi\in\mathcal{S}, i∗​(ξ)=p∗​(ξ′)i^{*}(\xi)=p^{*}(\xi^{\prime}), and v∈Γ​(F)v\in\Gamma(F). Then

[v,πM​(ξ)+ξ]|X=[v,πM​(ξ)]|X∈Γ​(F),\displaystyle[v,\pi_{M}(\xi)+\xi]|_{X}=[v,\pi_{M}(\xi)]|_{X}\in\Gamma(F),

since p∗​πM​(ξ)=πY​(ξ′)p_{*}\pi_{M}(\xi)=\pi_{Y}(\xi^{\prime}). Because Γ​(E∘)\Gamma(E^{\circ}) is spanned over C∞​(X)C^{\infty}(X) by 𝒮\mathcal{S}, it follows from the Leibniz rule for the Dorfman bracket that

[Γ​(F),Γ​(ℛπM​(E∘))]⊂Γ​(F)⊕Γ​(ℛπM​(E∘))\displaystyle[\Gamma(F),\Gamma(\mathcal{R}_{\pi_{M}}(E^{\circ}))]\subset\Gamma(F)\oplus\Gamma(\mathcal{R}_{\pi_{M}}(E^{\circ}))

and so Wit2) holds. ∎

Proposition 24 (CR3 holds).

If X=MX=M, a Dirac structure LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction to YY exactly when the vertical bundle to p:M→Yp:M\to Y is a witness for LML_{M}.

Proof.

When X=MX=M, we have that:

  • •

    E=FE=F;

  • •

    Wit3) is always met, since N∗​X⟂=𝕋​M|XN^{*}X^{\perp}=\mathbb{T}M|_{X};

  • •

    Wit1) is equivalent to LM​[F]L_{M}[F] being smooth;

  • •

    Wit2) is equivalent to LM​[F]L_{M}[F] involutive.

Therefore FF is a witness iff LM​[F]L_{M}[F] is a Dirac structure, and by Lemma 13, this is the case exactly when p!​(LM)p_{!}(L_{M}) is a Dirac structure on YY. ∎

Our proposed reduction scheme for a Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM to reduce under (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) to a Dirac structure on YY is that the ambient Dirac structure have a witness (𝔈\mathfrak{E}).
Remark 25.

It must be emphasized that the reduced Dirac structure on YY is the very same as that given by Theorem 14: because of (6), the map

ℰ:Lag​(𝕋​M)→Lag​(𝕋​M|X),\displaystyle\mathcal{E}:\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M)\to\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M|_{X}), ℰ​(L):=L​[E]​[N∗​X].\displaystyle\mathcal{E}(L):=L[E][N^{*}X].

coincides with LM↦LM​[I]L_{M}\mapsto L_{M}[I] if (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) is an adapted subbundle satisfying Wit3). The role of witnesses to the Dirac reduction is to ensure that concurrence is preserved, as we explain in the following section. It should also be pointed out that Dirac reduction does not necessarily admit a witness, as the next example shows.

Example 26.

Let ℱ\mathscr{F} be a foliation on a smooth manifold YY, and consider the triangle

M:=T∗​Y⟵iX:=N∗​ℱ⟶pY,\displaystyle M:=T^{*}Y\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i}}{{\longleftarrow}}X:=N^{*}\mathscr{F}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}Y,

where ii stands for the inclusion, and pp the canonical projection. Equip these manifolds with the Dirac structures

LM:=Gr​(ωcan),\displaystyle L_{M}:=\mathrm{Gr}(\omega_{\mathrm{can}}), LX:=Gr​(ωℱ),\displaystyle L_{X}:=\mathrm{Gr}(\omega_{\mathscr{F}}), LY:=Gr​(ℱ),\displaystyle L_{Y}:=\mathrm{Gr}(\mathscr{F}),

where ωcan\omega_{\mathrm{can}} is the canonical symplectic form on MM, and ωℱ:=i∗​(ωcan)\omega_{\mathscr{F}}:=i^{*}(\omega_{\mathrm{can}}). Then

i:(X,LX)→(M,LM)\displaystyle i:(X,L_{X})\to(M,L_{M})

is backward and

p:(X,LX)→(Y,LY)\displaystyle p:(X,L_{X})\to(Y,L_{Y})

is strong and forward — namely, every aY∈LYa_{Y}\in L_{Y} is pp-related to a unique aX∈LXa_{X}\in L_{X} [26, Example 6]. In particular, LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction to LYL_{Y} on YY. Note however that this Dirac reduction cannot be witnessed: indeed, by Proposition 23, the reduced Dirac structure on YY would be Poisson if this Dirac reduction were to admit a witness EE — and that only happens if ℱ\mathscr{F} is the foliation LY=T∗​YL_{Y}=T^{*}Y by points of YY.

Remark 27.

When ii in (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) is an embedding, and a Dirac structure LML_{M} has a witness (𝔈\mathfrak{E}), the embedded submanifold XX has a Dirac structure induced from (M,LM​[E])(M,L_{M}[E]), and the restriction of ii to an open, dense set U⊂XU\subset X splits it (Definition 11). Indeed, if UU is the maximal open set on which LM​[E]∩N∗​XL_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}X is smooth, then a smooth subbundle D⊂LM​[E]∩N∗​U⟂D\subset L_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}U^{\perp} exists, such that

LM​[E]​[N∗​U]=D⊕N∗​U,\displaystyle L_{M}[E][N^{*}U]=D\oplus N^{*}U,

so we may invoke Lemma 12 over UU. This allows us to relate certain sections of the reduced structure LYL_{Y} to those on LML_{M}, as we do in the sequel.

7. Compatibility with concurrence

In this section, we discuss the main feature of the extension of Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction to Dirac structures: namely, that it is compatible with (weak) concurrence.
Theorem 28.

If (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) is a witness for two weakly concurring Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} on MM, then the reduced Dirac structures LYL_{Y} and RYR_{Y} on YY concur weakly as well.

Proof.

Throughout the proof, given sections a∈Γ​(L)a\in\Gamma(L) and b∈Γ​(R)b\in\Gamma(R), we will say that aa and bb are ⊛\circledast-composable if prT∗​(a)=prT∗​(b)\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(a)=\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(b), in which case a⊛ba\circledast b denotes the section

a⊛b=a+prT​(b)=prT​(a)+b∈Γ​(L⊛R).\displaystyle a\circledast b=a+\mathrm{pr}_{T}(b)=\mathrm{pr}_{T}(a)+b\in\Gamma(L\circledast R).

Let EE be a common witness for two Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} on MM which concur weakly. Let LYL_{Y} and RYR_{Y} be the respective reduced Dirac structures on YY as in Theorem 14.

Step 0. We start with the observation that E⊂T​M|XE\subset TM|_{X} is a witness for two weakly concurring Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} on MM exactly when, for all connected, open set V⊂XV\subset X that ii embeds, we have that E|V⊂T​M|VE|_{V}\subset TM|_{V} is a witness for LML_{M} and RMR_{M}. It therefore suffices to argue the case where ii is an embedding.

Assuming that XX is an embedded submanifold, our task is to show that, for any aY,bY∈Γ​(LY⊛RY)a_{Y},b_{Y}\in\Gamma(L_{Y}\circledast R_{Y}), the bracket [aY,bY]∈Γ​(LY⊛RY)[a_{Y},b_{Y}]\in\Gamma(L_{Y}\circledast R_{Y}). Having chosen such sections, we will construct, in a series of steps, sections

aX,bXofp!​(LY)⊛p!​(RY),\displaystyle a_{X},b_{X}\ \ \text{of}\ \ p^{!}(L_{Y})\circledast p^{!}(R_{Y}), aM,bMofLM⊛RM,\displaystyle a_{M},b_{M}\ \ \text{of}\ \ L_{M}\circledast R_{M},

with

aX∼iaM,\displaystyle a_{X}\sim_{i}a_{M}, bX∼ibM,\displaystyle b_{X}\sim_{i}b_{M}, aX∼paY,\displaystyle a_{X}\sim_{p}a_{Y}, bX∼pbY.\displaystyle b_{X}\sim_{p}b_{Y}.

Step 1. There exists UYU_{Y} open and dense in YY such that

aY=aY,R⊛aY,L,\displaystyle a_{Y}=a_{Y,R}\circledast a_{Y,L}, bY=bY,R⊛bY,L,\displaystyle b_{Y}=b_{Y,R}\circledast b_{Y,L},

where

aY,L,bY,L∈Γ​(LY|UY),\displaystyle a_{Y,L},b_{Y,L}\in\Gamma(L_{Y}|_{U_{Y}}), aY,R,bY,R∈Γ​(RY|UY).\displaystyle a_{Y,R},b_{Y,R}\in\Gamma(R_{Y}|_{U_{Y}}).

To see that, observe that the cotangent product LY⊛RYL_{Y}\circledast R_{Y} can be described as follows: there is a canonical vector bundle map

δ:LY⊕RY→T∗​Y,\displaystyle\delta:L_{Y}\oplus R_{Y}\to T^{*}Y, δ​(aL,aR)=prT∗​(aL−aR),\displaystyle\delta(a_{L},a_{R})=\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(a_{L}-a_{R}),

and LY⊛RYL_{Y}\circledast R_{Y} is the image of the restriction to ker⁡(δ)⊂LY⊕RY\ker(\delta)\subset L_{Y}\oplus R_{Y} of the vector bundle map

ι:LY⊕RY→𝕋​Y,\displaystyle\iota:L_{Y}\oplus R_{Y}\to\mathbb{T}Y, ι​(aL,aR)=aL+prT​(aR).\displaystyle\iota(a_{L},a_{R})=a_{L}+\mathrm{pr}_{T}(a_{R}).

It then follows that, on the open, dense set UYU_{Y} on which δ\delta has locally constant rank, LY⊛RYL_{Y}\circledast R_{Y} is smooth (as the image under an injective map of a vector subbundle), and we may use

ι−1:(LY⊛RY)|UY→LY|UY⊕RY|UY\displaystyle\iota^{-1}:(L_{Y}\circledast R_{Y})|_{U_{Y}}\to L_{Y}|_{U_{Y}}\oplus R_{Y}|_{U_{Y}}

to construct a map of sections

Γ​((LY⊛RY)|UY)→Γ​(LY|UY)×Γ​(RY|UY),\displaystyle\Gamma((L_{Y}\circledast R_{Y})|_{U_{Y}})\to\Gamma(L_{Y}|_{U_{Y}})\times\Gamma(R_{Y}|_{U_{Y}}), aY↦(aY,L,aY,R),\displaystyle a_{Y}\mapsto(a_{Y,L},a_{Y,R}),

with the property that prT∗​(aY,L)=prT∗​(aY,R)\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(a_{Y,L})=\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(a_{Y,R}) and

aY=aY,L⊛aY,R:=aY,L+prT​(aY,R).\displaystyle a_{Y}=a_{Y,L}\circledast a_{Y,R}:=a_{Y,L}+\mathrm{pr}_{T}(a_{Y,R}).

Step 2. Choose an Ehresmann connection h:𝔛​(Y)→𝔛​(X).h:\mathfrak{X}(Y)\to\mathfrak{X}(X). Then we can define

aX,L†:=h​(prT​(aY,L))+p∗​(prT∗​(aY,L))∈Γ​(p!​(LY)|UX),\displaystyle a_{X,L}^{\dagger}:=h(\mathrm{pr}_{T}(a_{Y,L}))+p^{*}(\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(a_{Y,L}))\in\Gamma(p^{!}(L_{Y})|_{U_{X}}),

where UX:=p−1​(UY)U_{X}:=p^{-1}(U_{Y}). Observe that, by construction, aX,L†a_{X,L}^{\dagger} is pp-related to aY,La_{Y,L}. Using the same Ehresmann connection, construct analogously

bX,L†∈Γ​(p!​(LY)|UX),\displaystyle b_{X,L}^{\dagger}\in\Gamma(p^{!}(L_{Y})|_{U_{X}}), aX,R†,bX,R†∈Γ​(p!​(RY)|UX),\displaystyle a_{X,R}^{\dagger},b_{X,R}^{\dagger}\in\Gamma(p^{!}(R_{Y})|_{U_{X}}),

respectively pp-related to the sections bY,Lb_{Y,L}, aY,Ra_{Y,R} and bY,Rb_{Y,R}.

Step 3. Since EE is a common witness, we have that

i!​(LM​[E])=p!​(LY),\displaystyle i^{!}(L_{M}[E])=p^{!}(L_{Y}), i!​(RM​[E])=p!​(RY).\displaystyle i^{!}(R_{M}[E])=p^{!}(R_{Y}).

Therefore the four sections constructed in Step 2 can be regarded as sections

aX,L†,bX,L†∈Γ​(i!​(LM​[E])|UX),\displaystyle a_{X,L}^{\dagger},b_{X,L}^{\dagger}\in\Gamma(i^{!}(L_{M}[E])|_{U_{X}}), aX,R†,bX,R†∈Γ​(i!​(RM​[E])|UX).\displaystyle a_{X,R}^{\dagger},b_{X,R}^{\dagger}\in\Gamma(i^{!}(R_{M}[E])|_{U_{X}}).

Fix a linear splitting E=F⊕CE=F\oplus C, and observe that the canonical map T∗​M|X→T∗​XT^{*}M|_{X}\to T^{*}X restricts to a surjective map C∘→T∗​XC^{\circ}\to T^{*}X. Let λ:T∗​X→C∘\lambda:T^{*}X\to C^{\circ} be a linear splitting, and define a map

φ:Γ​(𝕋​UX)→Γ​(𝕋​M|UX),\displaystyle\varphi:\Gamma(\mathbb{T}U_{X})\to\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M|_{U_{X}}), a↦prT​(a)+λ​(prT∗​(a)),\displaystyle a\mapsto\mathrm{pr}_{T}(a)+\lambda\left(\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(a)\right),

and denote with aX,L‡:=φ​(aX,L†)a_{X,L}^{\ddagger}:=\varphi(a_{X,L}^{\dagger}) and analogously for the other sections. Then observe that the sections corresponding to aX,L†,bX,L†,aX,R†,bX,R†a_{X,L}^{\dagger},b_{X,L}^{\dagger},a_{X,R}^{\dagger},b_{X,R}^{\dagger} lie respectively in

aX,L‡,bX,L‡∈Γ​(LM​[E]|UX),\displaystyle a_{X,L}^{\ddagger},b_{X,L}^{\ddagger}\in\Gamma(L_{M}[E]|_{U_{X}}), aX,R‡,bX,R‡∈Γ​(RM​[E]|UX),\displaystyle a_{X,R}^{\ddagger},b_{X,R}^{\ddagger}\in\Gamma(R_{M}[E]|_{U_{X}}),

and are by construction ii-related to the original sections: aX,L†∼iaX,L‡a_{X,L}^{\dagger}\sim_{i}a_{X,L}^{\ddagger} and so on. Furthermore, note that there are well-defined sections

aX,L‡⊛aX,R‡,bX,L‡⊛bX,R‡∈Γ​(LM​[E]⊛RM​[E]|UX).\displaystyle a_{X,L}^{\ddagger}\circledast a_{X,R}^{\ddagger},\ \ b_{X,L}^{\ddagger}\circledast b_{X,R}^{\ddagger}\ \in\ \Gamma(L_{M}[E]\circledast R_{M}[E]|_{U_{X}}).

Step 4. Since

LM​[E]=LM∩E⟂+E\displaystyle L_{M}[E]=L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}+E RM​[E]=RM∩E⟂+E,\displaystyle R_{M}[E]=R_{M}\cap E^{\perp}+E,

there exists an open dense subset UX′U^{\prime}_{X} in UXU_{X} over which LM∩EL_{M}\cap E and RM∩ER_{M}\cap E are vector subbundles. Therefore one can find linear splittings

LM​[E]|UX′=(LM∩E⟂)|UX′⊕CL,\displaystyle L_{M}[E]|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}=(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp})|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}\oplus C_{L}, RM​[E]|UX′=(RM∩E⟂)|UX′⊕CR\displaystyle R_{M}[E]|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}=(R_{M}\cap E^{\perp})|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}\oplus C_{R}

where CLC_{L} and CRC_{R} are vector subbundles of E|UX′E|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}. Denote by

qL:LM​[E]|UX′→(LM∩E⟂)|UX′,\displaystyle q_{L}:L_{M}[E]|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}\to(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp})|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}, qR:RM​[E]|UX′→(RM∩E⟂)|UX′\displaystyle q_{R}:R_{M}[E]|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}\to(R_{M}\cap E^{\perp})|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}

the canonical projections, and define new sections

aX,L,bX,L∈Γ​(LM∩E⟂|UX′),\displaystyle a_{X,L},b_{X,L}\in\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}), aX,R,bX,R∈Γ​(RM∩E⟂|UX′)\displaystyle a_{X,R},b_{X,R}\in\Gamma(R_{M}\cap E^{\perp}|_{U^{\prime}_{X}})

by applying qLq_{L} to aX,L‡a_{X,L}^{\ddagger} and bX,L‡b_{X,L}^{\ddagger}, and qRq_{R} to aX,R‡a_{X,R}^{\ddagger} and bX,R‡b_{X,R}^{\ddagger}. Observe that

aX,L∼paY,L,\displaystyle a_{X,L}\sim_{p}a_{Y,L}, bX,L∼pbY,L,\displaystyle b_{X,L}\sim_{p}b_{Y,L}, aX,R∼paY,R,\displaystyle a_{X,R}\sim_{p}a_{Y,R}, bX,R∼pbY,R,\displaystyle b_{X,R}\sim_{p}b_{Y,R},

and

aX,L​and​aX,R​are ⊛-composable,\displaystyle a_{X,L}\ \text{and}\ a_{X,R}\ \text{are $\circledast$-composable}, bX,L​and​bX,R​are ⊛-composable.\displaystyle b_{X,L}\ \text{and}\ b_{X,R}\ \text{are $\circledast$-composable}.

Step 5. Because XX is embedded in MM, there is a tubular neighborhood for XX in MM, that is, an open set V⊂MV\subset M and a surjective submersion with connected fibres q:V→Xq:V\to X. Let h′:𝔛​(X)→𝔛​(V)h^{\prime}:\mathfrak{X}(X)\to\mathfrak{X}(V) be an Ehresmann connection for qq, whose horizontal distribution, restricted to points of XX coincides with T​X⊂T​V|XTX\subset TV|_{X}. Define as in Step 2 sections

aM,L,bM,L∈Γ​(LM|UM),\displaystyle a_{M,L},b_{M,L}\in\Gamma(L_{M}|_{U_{M}}), aM,R,bM,R∈Γ​(RM|UM),\displaystyle a_{M,R},b_{M,R}\in\Gamma(R_{M}|_{U_{M}}),

where UM:=q−1​(UX′)⊂VU_{M}:=q^{-1}(U^{\prime}_{X})\subset V. Then observe that

aX,L∼iaM,L,\displaystyle a_{X,L}\sim_{i}a_{M,L}, bX,L∼ibM,L,\displaystyle b_{X,L}\sim_{i}b_{M,L}, aX,R∼iaM,R,\displaystyle a_{X,R}\sim_{i}a_{M,R}, bX,R∼ibM,R,\displaystyle b_{X,R}\sim_{i}b_{M,R},

and

aM,L​and​aM,R​are ⊛-composable,\displaystyle a_{M,L}\ \text{and}\ a_{M,R}\ \text{are $\circledast$-composable}, bM,L​and​bM,R​are ⊛-composable.\displaystyle b_{M,L}\ \text{and}\ b_{M,R}\ \text{are $\circledast$-composable}.

Step 6. Because the bracket of ii-related sections is ii-related, we have that

[aM,bM]∈Γ​((LM⊛RM)|UM)\displaystyle[a_{M},b_{M}]\in\Gamma\left((L_{M}\circledast R_{M})|_{U_{M}}\right) implies [aX,bX]∈Γ​((p!​(LY)⊛p!​(RY))|UX′)\displaystyle[a_{X},b_{X}]\in\Gamma\left((p^{!}(L_{Y})\circledast p^{!}(R_{Y}))|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}\right)

where

aM:=aM,L⊛aM,R,\displaystyle a_{M}:=a_{M,L}\circledast a_{M,R}, bM:=bM,L⊛bM,R,\displaystyle b_{M}:=b_{M,L}\circledast b_{M,R}, aX:=aX,L⊛aX,R,\displaystyle a_{X}:=a_{X,L}\circledast a_{X,R}, bX:=bX,L⊛bX,R.\displaystyle b_{X}:=b_{X,L}\circledast b_{X,R}.

Likewise, the bracket of pp-related sections is pp-related, and so

[aX,bX]∈Γ​((p!​(LY)⊛p!​(RY))|UX′)\displaystyle[a_{X},b_{X}]\in\Gamma\left((p^{!}(L_{Y})\circledast p^{!}(R_{Y}))|_{U^{\prime}_{X}}\right) implies [aY,bY]|p​(UX′)∈Γ​((LY⊛RY)|p​(UX′)).\displaystyle[a_{Y},b_{Y}]|_{p(U^{\prime}_{X})}\in\Gamma\left((L_{Y}\circledast R_{Y})|_{p(U^{\prime}_{X})}\right).

Because p​(UX′)⊂Yp(U^{\prime}_{X})\subset Y is open and dense, we conclude that

[aY,bY]​ is a smooth section of ​LY⊛RY.\displaystyle[a_{Y},b_{Y}]\text{ is a smooth section of }L_{Y}\circledast R_{Y}.

This concludes the proof. ∎

Remark 29.

There is another natural guess of a reduction scheme for (𝔗\mathfrak{T}). Given a Dirac structure LL, suppose that there is an involutive subbundle CL⊂𝕋​M|XC_{L}\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{X} such that

L​[I]=CL⊕I.\displaystyle L[I]=C_{L}\oplus I.

This condition ensures that L​[I]L[I] is a Dirac structure; hence, by Theorem 14, LL reduces to a Dirac structure on YY. However, concurrence is not respected. For instance, for the Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} and I=N∗​XI=N^{*}X of Example 16, we have

L​[I]=CL⊕N∗​X,\displaystyle L[I]=C_{L}\oplus N^{*}X, CL=⟨∂∂x1,d​x2,d​x3⟩\displaystyle C_{L}=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\mathrm{d}x_{2},\mathrm{d}x_{3}\right\rangle
R​[I]=CR⊕N∗​X,\displaystyle R[I]=C_{R}\oplus N^{*}X, CR=⟨d​x1,∂∂x2+x1​∂∂x3,x1​d​x2−d​x3⟩,\displaystyle C_{R}=\left\langle\mathrm{d}x_{1},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}},x_{1}\mathrm{d}x_{2}-\mathrm{d}x_{3}\right\rangle,

which are involutive. Nevertheless, the reduced (i.e., induced) Dirac structures on XX do not concur.

8. Reduction by kernels

Here we switch our attention to the following question: given concurring Dirac structures LL and RR, are there recipes to construct witnesses EE for both structures? Our main contribution to this question is the observation that the kernels LM∩T​ML_{M}\cap TM and RM∩T​MR_{M}\cap TM are simultaneous witnesses whenever smooth. We even conjecture that all (known) recipes are of this kind, in one way or another.

Let LML_{M} be a Dirac structure on MM, and let

K​(LM):=LM∩T​M\displaystyle K(L_{M}):=L_{M}\cap TM

denote its kernel. If K​(LM)K(L_{M}) is a vector bundle, it defines a foliation, and if that foliation is simple, and is defined by a surjective submersion

p:M→Y,\displaystyle p:M\to Y,

then by Lemma 13, p!​(LM)p_{!}(L_{M}) is a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY (since LM​[K​(LM)]=LML_{M}[K(L_{M})]=L_{M}), and, in fact, LYL_{Y} is the graph of a Poisson structure, since LM∩K​(LM)=K​(LM)L_{M}\cap K(L_{M})=K(L_{M}) implies that LY∩T​Y=0L_{Y}\cap TY=0. In short, we may say that

Every Dirac structure reduces by its kernel to a Poisson structure.

Our next main result illustrates a rather surprising fact: that, modulo smoothness issues,

A Dirac structure reduces by the kernel of any Dirac structure with which it concurs.

Theorem 30.

Let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} be weakly concurring Dirac structures. Then

LM,\displaystyle L_{M}, RM,\displaystyle R_{M}, Gr​(K​(LM)),\displaystyle\mathrm{Gr}(K(L_{M})), Gr​(K​(RM))\displaystyle\mathrm{Gr}(K(R_{M}))

all concur weakly, where Gr​(K​(LM))\mathrm{Gr}(K(L_{M})) and Gr​(K​(RM))\mathrm{Gr}(K(R_{M})) are the involutive Lagrangian families corresponding to

K​(LM)=LM∩T​M,\displaystyle K(L_{M})=L_{M}\cap TM, K​(RM)=RM∩T​M.\displaystyle K(R_{M})=R_{M}\cap TM.
Proof.

Note that

LM​[K​(RM)]\displaystyle L_{M}[K(R_{M})] =LM∩K​(RM)⟂+K​(RM),\displaystyle=L_{M}\cap K(R_{M})^{\perp}+K(R_{M}),

is involutive if

LM∩K​(RM)⟂={u+ξ∈LM|ξ∈K​(RM)∘}\displaystyle L_{M}\cap K(R_{M})^{\perp}=\{u+\xi\in L_{M}\ |\ \xi\in K(R_{M})^{\circ}\}

is involutive. Now, because LM∩K​(RM)⟂⊂LM⊛RML_{M}\cap K(R_{M})^{\perp}\subset L_{M}\circledast R_{M}, we have that

[Γ​(LM∩K​(RM)⟂),Γ​(LM∩K​(RM)⟂)]⊂Γ​(LM)∩Γ​(LM⊛RM),\displaystyle\left[\Gamma(L_{M}\cap K(R_{M})^{\perp}),\Gamma(L_{M}\cap K(R_{M})^{\perp})\right]\subset\Gamma(L_{M})\cap\Gamma(L_{M}\circledast R_{M}),

and the latter lies inside Γ​(LM∩K​(RM)⟂)\Gamma(L_{M}\cap K(R_{M})^{\perp}). Thus LM​[K​(RM)]L_{M}[K(R_{M})] is involutive, and, symmetrically, RM​[K​(LM)]R_{M}[K(L_{M})] is also involutive. Finally,

K​(LM)+K​(RM)=(LM⊛RM)∩T​M\displaystyle K(L_{M})+K(R_{M})=\left(L_{M}\circledast R_{M}\right)\cap TM

is involutive because LM⊛RML_{M}\circledast R_{M} and T​MTM are involutive. ∎

Corollary 31.

Let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} be weakly concurring Dirac structures on MM, and suppose there are surjective submersions

such that

ker⁡pL∗=K​(LM),\displaystyle\ker{p_{L}}_{*}=K(L_{M}), ker⁡pR∗=K​(RM),\displaystyle\ker{p_{R}}_{*}=K(R_{M}), ker⁡qL∗=KK​(LM),\displaystyle\ker{q_{L}}_{*}=\frac{K}{K(L_{M})}, ker⁡qR∗=KK​(RM)\displaystyle\ker{q_{R}}_{*}=\frac{K}{K(R_{M})}

where K:=K​(LM)+K​(RM)K:=K(L_{M})+K(R_{M}). Then LML_{M}, RMR_{M} push forward, under each of these submersions, to weakly concurring Dirac structures, and

pL!​(L),\displaystyle{p_{L}}_{!}(L), pR!​(R),\displaystyle{p_{R}}_{!}(R), r!​(L),\displaystyle{r}_{!}(L), r!​(R)\displaystyle{r}_{!}(R)

are Poisson.

Example 32.

Let M=ℝ5M=\mathbb{R}^{5} be equipped with the closed two-forms

ωL=d​x2∧d​x3+d​x4∧d​x5,\displaystyle\omega_{L}=\mathrm{d}x_{2}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{3}+\mathrm{d}x_{4}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{5}, ωR=d​x1∧d​x2+d​x3∧d​x4,\displaystyle\omega_{R}=\mathrm{d}x_{1}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{2}+\mathrm{d}x_{3}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{4},

and let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} denote the respective graphs. They concur since

LM⊛RM=⟨∂∂x1,∂∂x2−∂∂x4+d​x3,∂∂x3−d​x2,∂∂x3+d​x4,∂∂x5⟩\displaystyle L_{M}\circledast R_{M}=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}-\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}+\mathrm{d}x_{3},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}-\mathrm{d}x_{2},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}+\mathrm{d}x_{4},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}}\right\rangle

is Dirac. We have a diamond

and the Dirac structures implied in Corollary 31 are

LMK​(L)=Gr​(d​x2∧d​x3+d​x4∧d​x5),\displaystyle L_{M_{K(L)}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\mathrm{d}x_{2}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{3}+\mathrm{d}x_{4}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{5}), RMK​(L)=⟨d​x2,∂∂x3+d​x4,∂∂x4−d​x3,∂∂x5⟩\displaystyle R_{M_{K(L)}}=\left\langle\mathrm{d}x_{2},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}+\mathrm{d}x_{4},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}-\mathrm{d}x_{3},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}}\right\rangle
LMK​(R)=⟨∂∂x1,∂∂x2+d​x3,∂∂x3−d​x2,d​x4⟩,\displaystyle L_{M_{K(R)}}=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+\mathrm{d}x_{3},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}-\mathrm{d}x_{2},\mathrm{d}x_{4}\right\rangle, RMK​(R)=Gr​(d​x1∧d​x2+d​x3∧d​x4)\displaystyle R_{M_{K(R)}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\mathrm{d}x_{1}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{2}+\mathrm{d}x_{3}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{4})
LMK​(L​R)=Gr​(∂∂x3∧∂∂x2),\displaystyle L_{M_{K(LR)}}=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}\right), RMK​(L​R)=Gr​(∂∂x4∧∂∂x3).\displaystyle R_{M_{K(LR)}}=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\right).

Clearly,

LMK​(L​R)⊛RMK​(L​R)=Gr​((∂∂x4−∂∂x2)∧∂∂x3).\displaystyle L_{M_{K(LR)}}\circledast R_{M_{K(LR)}}=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}-\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}\right)\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\right).

is again Dirac.

Remark 33.

To illustrate why concurrence is such a strong compatibility condition, suppose two closed two-forms ωL,ωR∈Ω2​(M)\omega_{L},\omega_{R}\in\Omega^{2}(M) concur weakly, and that ker⁡(ωR)\ker(\omega_{R}) is a vector bundle (and thus, a foliation). Then ωL\omega_{L} and ωϵ:=ϵ​ωL+ωR\omega_{\epsilon}:=\epsilon\omega_{L}+\omega_{R} concur weakly, for all ϵ∈ℝ\epsilon\in\mathbb{R}; hence by Theorem 30, wherever smooth, ker⁡(ωϵ)\ker(\omega_{\epsilon}) is a witness ωL\omega_{L}. This indicates that concurring, closed two-forms should be in short supply, and is reminiscent of the results in, e.g.,[5].

9. Magri’s recipe

In [7], the authors consider two commuting Poisson structures πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R} on a manifold MM, and take X⊂MX\subset M to be a leaf of πR\pi_{R}. Then they show that, if

EL:=πL​(ker⁡πR)|X⊂T​M|X\displaystyle E_{L}:=\pi_{L}(\ker\pi_{R})|_{X}\subset TM|_{X}

and EL∩T​XE_{L}\cap TX have locally constant rank, then ELE_{L} is a witness for both πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R}. In fact we can also take X=MX=M [9] and state, more symmetrically, that (under the same constant rank conditions),

(7) EL:=πL​(ker⁡πR),\displaystyle E_{L}:=\pi_{L}(\ker\pi_{R}), ER:=πR​(ker⁡πL)\displaystyle E_{R}:=\pi_{R}(\ker\pi_{L})

are simultaneously witnesses for πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R}. We refer to (7) as Magri’s original recipe. Below, we generalize Magri’s original recipe to general weakly concurring Dirac structures.

Consider the function of Lagrangian families

𝒩\displaystyle\mathscr{N} :Lag​(𝕋​M)×Lag​(𝕋​M)→Lag​(𝕋​M),\displaystyle:\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M)\times\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M)\to\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M),
𝒩​(L,R)\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(L,R) :=L⋆(R⊛ℛ−1​(L)).\displaystyle:=L\star\left(R\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(L)\right).
Definition 34.

We call Magri’s recipe the function

ℳ\displaystyle\mathscr{M} :Lag​(𝕋​M)×Lag​(𝕋​M)→Lag​(𝕋​M),\displaystyle:\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M)\times\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M)\to\mathrm{Lag}(\mathbb{T}M),
ℳ​(L,R)\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(L,R) :=Gr​(K​(𝒩​(L,R))).\displaystyle:=\mathrm{Gr}(K(\mathscr{N}(L,R))).
Example 35.

When LL and RR are, respectively, the graphs of Poisson structures πL,πR∈𝔛2​(M)\pi_{L},\pi_{R}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M), then

𝒩​(L,R)\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(L,R) =Gr​(πL)⋆Gr​(πR−πL)\displaystyle=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{L})\star\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{R}-\pi_{L})
={πL​(ξ)+ξ|ξ∈T∗​M}⋆{πR​(η)−πL​(η)+η|η∈T∗​M}\displaystyle=\{\pi_{L}(\xi)+\xi\ |\ \xi\in T^{*}M\}\star\{\pi_{R}(\eta)-\pi_{L}(\eta)+\eta\ |\ \eta\in T^{*}M\}
={πL​(ξ)+ξ+η|ξ,η∈T∗​M,πL​(ξ+η)=πR​(η)}.\displaystyle=\{\pi_{L}(\xi)+\xi+\eta\ |\ \xi,\eta\in T^{*}M,\ \pi_{L}(\xi+\eta)=\pi_{R}(\eta)\}.

Therefore ℳ​(L,R)\mathscr{M}(L,R) is the graph of

EL\displaystyle E_{L} ={πL​(ξ)|ξ∈T∗​M,πR​(ξ)=0}=πL​(ker⁡πR).\displaystyle=\{\pi_{L}(\xi)\ |\ \xi\in T^{*}M,\ \pi_{R}(\xi)=0\}=\pi_{L}(\ker\pi_{R}).

Hence Magri’s recipe ℳ​(L,R)\mathscr{M}(L,R) coincides with (the graph of) Magri’s original recipe (7) when the Dirac structures in question are Poisson.

Theorem 36.

Let LL and RR be weakly concurring Dirac structures on MM. Then the Magri recipes

ℳ​(L,R),\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(L,R), ℳ​(R,L)\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(R,L)

are involutive, and

L,\displaystyle L, R,\displaystyle R, ℳ​(L,R),\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(L,R), ℳ​(R,L)\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(R,L)

concur weakly pairwise.

Proof.

Let us first observe that, because LL and RR concur weakly, we have that R⊛ℛ−1​(L)R\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(L) is involutive, and therefore so is the tangent product 𝒩​(L,R):=L⋆(R⊛ℛ−1​(L))\mathscr{N}(L,R):=L\star(R\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(L)). Therefore EL:=𝒩​(L,R)∩T​ME_{L}:=\mathscr{N}(L,R)\cap TM is involutive; likewise, ER:=𝒩​(R,L)∩T​ME_{R}:=\mathscr{N}(R,L)\cap TM is involutive.

Let U⊂MU\subset M be the maximal open set on which

K​(L),\displaystyle K(L), K​(R),\displaystyle K(R), K​(L)+K​(R),\displaystyle K(L)+K(R), 𝒩​(L,R),\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(L,R), EL,\displaystyle E_{L}, 𝒩​(R,L),\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(R,L), ER\displaystyle E_{R}

are vector bundles. Then UU is dense in MM. It thus suffices to check that the theorem holds in an open neighborhood V⊂UV\subset U of every point x∈Ux\in U. We may thus assume that we are in the setting of Corollary 31 — that is, that there exists a commutative diagram of surjective submersions with connected fibres

in which

ker⁡pL∗=K​(L),\displaystyle\ker{p_{L}}_{*}=K(L), ker⁡pR∗=K​(R),\displaystyle\ker{p_{R}}_{*}=K(R), ker⁡qL∗=K/K​(R),\displaystyle\ker{q_{L}}_{*}=K/K(R), ker⁡qR∗=K/K​(L),\displaystyle\ker{q_{R}}_{*}=K/K(L),

where K:=K​(L)+K​(R)K:=K(L)+K(R), and there exist Poisson structures

πL∈𝔛2​(ML),\displaystyle\pi_{L}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M_{L}), πR∈𝔛2​(MR),\displaystyle\pi_{R}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M_{R}), νL∈𝔛2​(ML​R),\displaystyle\nu_{L}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M_{LR}), νR∈𝔛2​(ML​R),\displaystyle\nu_{R}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M_{LR}),

such that

L=pL!​Gr​(πL),\displaystyle L={p_{L}}^{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{L}), R=pR!​Gr​(πR),\displaystyle R={p_{R}}^{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{R}), r!​(L)=Gr​(νL),\displaystyle r_{!}(L)=\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{L}), r!​(R)=Gr​(νR).\displaystyle r_{!}(R)=\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{R}).

We may thus write

L=ℛΠL​Gr​(K​(L)),\displaystyle L=\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}\mathrm{Gr}(K(L)), R=ℛΠR​Gr​(K​(R)),\displaystyle R=\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{R}}\mathrm{Gr}(K(R)),

for two bivectors ΠL,ΠR∈𝔛2​(M)\Pi_{L},\Pi_{R}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M) for which

πL​(ξ)=(pL)∗​ΠL​((pL)∗​ξ),\displaystyle\pi_{L}(\xi)=(p_{L})_{*}\Pi_{L}((p_{L})^{*}\xi), ξ∈T∗​ML,\displaystyle\xi\in T^{*}M_{L},
πR​(η)=(pR)∗​ΠR​((pR)∗​η),\displaystyle\pi_{R}(\eta)=(p_{R})_{*}\Pi_{R}((p_{R})^{*}\eta), η∈T∗​MR.\displaystyle\eta\in T^{*}M_{R}.

Step 1. Description of ELE_{L} and ERE_{R}. Because

L\displaystyle L ={u+ΠL​(ξ)+ξ|u∈K​(L),ξ∈K​(L)∘}\displaystyle=\left\{u+\Pi_{L}(\xi)+\xi\ |\ u\in K(L),\ \xi\in K(L)^{\circ}\right\}
R⊛ℛ−1​(L)\displaystyle R\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(L) ={v+ΠR​(η)−ΠL​(η)+η|v∈K,η∈K∘}\displaystyle=\left\{v+\Pi_{R}(\eta)-\Pi_{L}(\eta)+\eta\ |\ v\in K,\ \eta\in K^{\circ}\right\}

we see that

𝒩​(L,R)\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(L,R) ={u+ΠL​(ξ)+ξ+η|u∈K​(L),v∈K,ξ∈K​(L)∘,η∈K∘,u+ΠL​(ξ+η)=v+ΠR​(η)}.\displaystyle=\left\{u+\Pi_{L}(\xi)+\xi+\eta\ \Bigg|\ \begin{aligned} u\in K(L),&\ v\in K,\\ \xi\in K(L)^{\circ},&\ \eta\in K^{\circ},\\ u+\Pi_{L}(\xi+\eta)&=v+\Pi_{R}(\eta)\end{aligned}\right\}.

Therefore

EL=K​(L)+ΠL​(HL),\displaystyle E_{L}=K(L)+\Pi_{L}(H_{L}), HL={ξ∈K∘|ΠR​(ξ)∈K}.\displaystyle H_{L}=\left\{\xi\in K^{\circ}\ |\ \Pi_{R}(\xi)\in K\right\}.

Observe that

HL=(R⊛Gr​(K))∩T∗​M,\displaystyle H_{L}=\left(R\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(K)\right)\cap T^{*}M,

and therefore

HL∘=prT​(R⊛Gr​(K))=K+ΠR​(K∘).\displaystyle H_{L}^{\circ}=\mathrm{pr}_{T}\left(R\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(K)\right)=K+\Pi_{R}(K^{\circ}).

This implies that

EL∘\displaystyle E_{L}^{\circ} =K​(L)∘∩ΠL−1​(HL∘)\displaystyle=K(L)^{\circ}\cap\Pi_{L}^{-1}(H_{L}^{\circ})
={ξ∈K​(L)∘|ΠL​(ξ)∈K+ΠR​(K∘)}\displaystyle=\left\{\xi\in K(L)^{\circ}\ |\ \Pi_{L}(\xi)\in K+\Pi_{R}(K^{\circ})\right\}

Likewise

ER=K​(R)+ΠR​(HR),\displaystyle E_{R}=K(R)+\Pi_{R}(H_{R}), ER∘\displaystyle E_{R}^{\circ} ={ξ∈K​(R)∘|ΠR​(ξ)∈K+ΠL​(K∘)}.\displaystyle=\left\{\xi\in K(R)^{\circ}\ |\ \Pi_{R}(\xi)\in K+\Pi_{L}(K^{\circ})\right\}.

Step 2. LL and ℳ​(L,R)\mathscr{M}(L,R) concur weakly. Because

L⊛ℳ​(L,R)\displaystyle L\circledast\mathscr{M}(L,R) =L∩EL⟂+EL\displaystyle=L\cap E_{L}^{\perp}+E_{L}

and ELE_{L} is involutive, in order to show that LL and ℳ​(L,R)\mathscr{M}(L,R) concur weakly, it suffices to show that

(8) L∩EL⟂\displaystyle L\cap E_{L}^{\perp} =K​(L)+ℛΠL​(EL∘)\displaystyle=K(L)+\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ})

is involutive. Because LL is involutive, we have that

  1. La)

    ΠL​[v,ξ]−[v,ΠL​(ξ)]∈Γ​(K​(L))\Pi_{L}[v,\xi]-[v,\Pi_{L}(\xi)]\in\Gamma(K(L)),

  2. Lb)

    [ξ,η]ΠL∈Γ​(K​(L)∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}\in\Gamma(K(L)^{\circ});

  3. Lc)

    ΠL​[ξ,η]ΠL−[ΠL​(ξ),ΠL​(η)]∈Γ​(K​(L))\Pi_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}-[\Pi_{L}(\xi),\Pi_{L}(\eta)]\in\Gamma(K(L))

for all v∈Γ​(K​(L))v\in\Gamma(K(L)) and all ξ,η∈Γ​(K​(L)∘)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(K(L)^{\circ}). Likewise, RR involutive implies

  1. Ra)

    ΠR​[v,ξ]−[v,ΠR​(ξ)]∈Γ​(K​(R))\Pi_{R}[v,\xi]-[v,\Pi_{R}(\xi)]\in\Gamma(K(R)),

  2. Rb)

    [ξ,η]ΠR∈Γ​(K​(R)∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}\in\Gamma(K(R)^{\circ});

  3. Rc)

    ΠR​[ξ,η]ΠR−[ΠR​(ξ),ΠR​(η)]∈Γ​(K​(R))\Pi_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}-[\Pi_{R}(\xi),\Pi_{R}(\eta)]\in\Gamma(K(R))

Moreover, because

L⊛Gr​(K)\displaystyle L\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(K) =K+ℛΠL​(K∘)\displaystyle=K+\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(K^{\circ})

is involutive by Theorem 30, we have that

  1. LKa)

    ΠL​[v,ξ]≡[v,ΠL​(ξ)]\Pi_{L}[v,\xi]\equiv[v,\Pi_{L}(\xi)],

  2. LKb)

    [ξ,η]ΠL∈Γ​(K∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ});

  3. LKc)

    ΠL​[ξ,η]ΠL≡[ΠL​(ξ),ΠL​(η)]\Pi_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}\equiv[\Pi_{L}(\xi),\Pi_{L}(\eta)]

for all v∈Γ​(K)v\in\Gamma(K) and ξ,η∈Γ​(K∘)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}), where we write ≡\equiv for equality up to Γ​(K)\Gamma(K). Likewise,

R⊛Gr​(K)\displaystyle R\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(K) =K+ℛΠR​(K∘)\displaystyle=K+\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{R}}(K^{\circ})

involutive implies that

  1. RKa)

    ΠR​[v,ξ]≡[v,ΠR​(ξ)]\Pi_{R}[v,\xi]\equiv[v,\Pi_{R}(\xi)],

  2. RKb)

    [ξ,η]ΠR∈Γ​(K∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ});

  3. RKc)

    ΠR​[ξ,η]ΠR≡[ΠR​(ξ),ΠR​(η)]\Pi_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}\equiv[\Pi_{R}(\xi),\Pi_{R}(\eta)]

We show that

(9) [Γ​(K​(L)),Γ​(ℛΠL​(EL∘))]⊂Γ​(K​(L))+Γ​(ℛΠL​(EL∘)).\displaystyle\left[\Gamma(K(L)),\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ})\right)\right]\subset\Gamma(K(L))+\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ})\right).

Let v∈K​(L)v\in K(L) and ξ∈Γ​(EL∘)\xi\in\Gamma(E_{L}^{\circ}). Then there exists ξ′∈Γ​(K∘)\xi^{\prime}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}), such that ΠL​(ξ)≡ΠR​(ξ′)\Pi_{L}(\xi)\equiv\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime}). Note that [v,ξ]∈Γ​(K​(L)∘)[v,\xi]\in\Gamma(K(L)^{\circ}) and [v,ξ′]∈Γ​(K∘)[v,\xi^{\prime}]\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}), and because

ΠL​[v,ξ]≡[v,ΠL​(ξ)]≡[v,ΠR​(ξ′)]≡ΠR​[v,ξ′]\displaystyle\Pi_{L}[v,\xi]\equiv[v,\Pi_{L}(\xi)]\equiv[v,\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime})]\equiv\Pi_{R}[v,\xi^{\prime}]

by La) and RKa), we deduce that, in fact, [v,ξ]∈Γ​(EL∘)[v,\xi]\in\Gamma(E_{L}^{\circ}). Therefore

[v,ΠL​(ξ)+ξ]≡ΠL​[v,ξ]+[v,ξ]∈Γ​(ℛΠL​(EL∘)).\displaystyle[v,\Pi_{L}(\xi)+\xi]\equiv\Pi_{L}[v,\xi]+[v,\xi]\in\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ})\right).

Hence (9) holds true. We show next that

(10) [Γ​(ℛΠL​(EL∘)),Γ​(ℛΠL​(EL∘))]⊂Γ​(K​(L))+Γ​(ℛΠL​(EL∘)).\displaystyle\left[\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ})\right),\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ})\right)\right]\subset\Gamma(K(L))+\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ})\right).

Let ξ,η∈Γ​(EL∘)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(E_{L}^{\circ}). Observe that, since EL∘⊂K​(L)∘E_{L}^{\circ}\subset K(L)^{\circ}, we have that [ξ,η]ΠL∈Γ​(K​(L)∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}\in\Gamma(K(L)^{\circ}) by Lb). Write now

ΠL​(ξ)=ΠR​(ξ′)+u,\displaystyle\Pi_{L}(\xi)=\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime})+u, ΠL​(η)=ΠR​(η′)+v,\displaystyle\Pi_{L}(\eta)=\Pi_{R}(\eta^{\prime})+v, ξ′,η′∈Γ​(K∘),\displaystyle\xi^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}), u,v∈Γ​(K).\displaystyle u,v\in\Gamma(K).

Then

ΠL​[ξ,η]ΠL\displaystyle\Pi_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}} ≡[ΠL​(ξ),ΠL​(η)]≡[ΠR​(ξ′)+u,ΠR​(η′)+v]\displaystyle\equiv[\Pi_{L}(\xi),\Pi_{L}(\eta)]\equiv[\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime})+u,\Pi_{R}(\eta^{\prime})+v]
≡[ΠR​(ξ′),ΠR​(η′)]+[u,ΠR​(η′)]+[ΠR​(ξ′),v]+[u,v]\displaystyle\equiv[\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime}),\Pi_{R}(\eta^{\prime})]+[u,\Pi_{R}(\eta^{\prime})]+[\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime}),v]+[u,v]
≡ΠR​([ξ′,η′]ΠR+[u,η′]+[ξ′,v])\displaystyle\equiv\Pi_{R}\left([\xi^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}]^{\Pi_{R}}+[u,\eta^{\prime}]+[\xi^{\prime},v]\right)

shows that [ξ,η]ΠL∈Γ​(EL∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}\in\Gamma(E_{L}^{\circ}), since [ξ′,η′]ΠR∈Γ​(K∘)[\xi^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}]^{\Pi_{R}}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}) by RKb). Therefore

[ΠL​(ξ)+ξ,ΠL​(η)+η]≡ΠL​[ξ,η]ΠL+[ξ,η]ΠL\displaystyle[\Pi_{L}(\xi)+\xi,\Pi_{L}(\eta)+\eta]\equiv\Pi_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}+[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}

shows that (10) holds true.

Step 3. RR and ℳ​(L,R)\mathscr{M}(L,R) concur weakly. As before,

R⊛ℳ​(L,R)\displaystyle R\circledast\mathscr{M}(L,R) =R∩EL⟂+EL\displaystyle=R\cap E_{L}^{\perp}+E_{L}

is involutive if

(11) R∩EL⟂\displaystyle R\cap E_{L}^{\perp} =K​(R)+ℛΠR​(K​(R)∘∩EL∘)\displaystyle=K(R)+\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{R}}(K(R)^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ})

is involutive. Let v∈Γ​(K​(R))v\in\Gamma(K(R)) and ξ∈Γ​(K​(R)∘∩EL∘)\xi\in\Gamma(K(R)^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ}). Because ξ∈Γ​(EL∘)\xi\in\Gamma(E_{L}^{\circ}),

ΠL​(ξ)=ΠR​(ξ′)+u,\displaystyle\Pi_{L}(\xi)=\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime})+u, ξ′∈Γ​(K∘),u∈Γ​(K).\displaystyle\xi^{\prime}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}),\ u\in\Gamma(K).

Then [v,ξ]∈Γ​(K​(R)∘)[v,\xi]\in\Gamma(K(R)^{\circ}), and because

ΠL​[v,ξ]≡[v,ΠL​(ξ)]≡[v,ΠR​(ξ′)+u]≡ΠR​[v,ξ′]\displaystyle\Pi_{L}[v,\xi]\equiv[v,\Pi_{L}(\xi)]\equiv[v,\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime})+u]\equiv\Pi_{R}[v,\xi^{\prime}]

we see that [v,ξ]∈Γ​(K​(R)∘∩EL∘)[v,\xi]\in\Gamma(K(R)^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ}). Therefore

(12) [v,ΠR​(ξ)+ξ]\displaystyle[v,\Pi_{R}(\xi)+\xi] ≡ΠR​[v,ξ]+[v,ξ]∈Γ​(ℛΠR​(K​(R)∘∩EL∘)).\displaystyle\equiv\Pi_{R}[v,\xi]+[v,\xi]\in\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{R}}(K(R)^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ})\right).
