Let next η∈Γ​(K∘∩EL∘)\eta\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ}). Then [ξ,η]ΠR∈Γ​(K∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}) by RKb). Because

L⊛R⊛Gr​(K)=K+ℛΠL+ΠR​(K∘)\displaystyle L\circledast R\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(K)=K+\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}+\Pi_{R}}(K^{\circ})

is involutive, we have that

(LRK) ΠL​[ξ,η]ΠR+ΠR​[ξ,η]ΠL≡[ΠL​(ξ),ΠR​(η)]+[ΠR​(ξ),ΠL​(η)]\displaystyle\Pi_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}+\Pi_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}\equiv[\Pi_{L}(\xi),\Pi_{R}(\eta)]+[\Pi_{R}(\xi),\Pi_{L}(\eta)]

for all ξ,η∈Γ​(K∘)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}). Therefore, if ξ,η∈Γ​(K∘∩EL∘)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ}), that is

ΠL​(ξ)=ΠR​(ξ′)+u,\displaystyle\Pi_{L}(\xi)=\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime})+u, ΠL​(η)=ΠR​(η′)+v,\displaystyle\Pi_{L}(\eta)=\Pi_{R}(\eta^{\prime})+v, ξ′,η′∈Γ​(K∘),\displaystyle\xi^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}), u,v∈Γ​(K),\displaystyle u,v\in\Gamma(K),

then

ΠL​[ξ,η]ΠR\displaystyle\Pi_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}} ≡[ΠL​(ξ),ΠR​(η)]+[ΠR​(ξ),ΠL​(η)]−ΠR​[ξ,η]ΠL\displaystyle\equiv[\Pi_{L}(\xi),\Pi_{R}(\eta)]+[\Pi_{R}(\xi),\Pi_{L}(\eta)]-\Pi_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}
≡[ΠR​(ξ′)+u,ΠR​(η)]+[ΠR​(ξ),ΠR​(η′)+v]−ΠR​[ξ,η]ΠL\displaystyle\equiv[\Pi_{R}(\xi^{\prime})+u,\Pi_{R}(\eta)]+[\Pi_{R}(\xi),\Pi_{R}(\eta^{\prime})+v]-\Pi_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}
≡ΠR​([ξ′,η]ΠR+[u,η]+[ξ,η′]ΠR+[ξ,v]−[ξ,η]ΠL)\displaystyle\equiv\Pi_{R}\left([\xi^{\prime},\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}+[u,\eta]+[\xi,\eta^{\prime}]^{\Pi_{R}}+[\xi,v]-[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{L}}\right)

shows that [ξ,η]ΠR∈Γ​(K∘∩EL∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}\in\Gamma(K^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ}). Therefore

(13) [ΠR​(ξ)+ξ,ΠR​(η)+η]\displaystyle[\Pi_{R}(\xi)+\xi,\Pi_{R}(\eta)+\eta] ≡ΠR​[ξ,η]ΠR+[ξ,η]ΠR∈Γ​(ℛΠR​(K∘∩EL∘))\displaystyle\equiv\Pi_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}+[\xi,\eta]^{\Pi_{R}}\in\Gamma\left(\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{R}}(K^{\circ}\cap E_{L}^{\circ})\right)

Then observe that (12) and (13) imply that (11) is involutive.

Step 4. LL and RR concur weakly with ℳ​(R,L)\mathscr{M}(R,L). Just reverse the roles of LL and RR in Steps 2 and 3.

Step 5. ℳ​(L,R)\mathscr{M}(L,R) and ℳ​(R,L)\mathscr{M}(R,L) concur weakly. We show that

E:=EL+ER⊂T​M\displaystyle E:=E_{L}+E_{R}\subset TM

is involutive. By Theorem 30, there exist Poisson structures νL,νR∈𝔛2​(ML​R)\nu_{L},\nu_{R}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M_{LR}), such that

r!​(L)=Gr​(νL),\displaystyle r_{!}(L)=\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{L}), r!​(R)=Gr​(νR).\displaystyle r_{!}(R)=\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{R}).

We can then write

HL=r∗​(ker⁡νR),\displaystyle H_{L}=r^{*}(\ker\nu_{R}), HR=r∗​(ker⁡νL),\displaystyle H_{R}=r^{*}(\ker\nu_{L}),

and therefore

E\displaystyle E =EL+ER=K+ΠL​r∗​(ker⁡νR)+ΠR​r∗​(ker⁡νL)\displaystyle=E_{L}+E_{R}=K+\Pi_{L}r^{*}(\ker\nu_{R})+\Pi_{R}r^{*}(\ker\nu_{L})
=r∗−1​(νL​(ker⁡(νR))+νR​(ker⁡(νL))).\displaystyle=r_{*}^{-1}\left(\nu_{L}(\ker(\nu_{R}))+\nu_{R}(\ker(\nu_{L}))\right).

So it suffices to show that νL​(ker⁡(νR))+νR​(ker⁡(νL))\nu_{L}(\ker(\nu_{R}))+\nu_{R}(\ker(\nu_{L})) is involutive. Because νL\nu_{L} and νR\nu_{R} commute by Theorem 28, we have that

(14) νL​[ξ,η]νR+νR​[ξ,η]νL=[νL​(ξ),νR​(η)]+[νR​(ξ),νL​(η)]\displaystyle\nu_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\nu_{R}}+\nu_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\nu_{L}}=[\nu_{L}(\xi),\nu_{R}(\eta)]+[\nu_{R}(\xi),\nu_{L}(\eta)]

for all ξ,η∈Ω1​(ML​R)\xi,\eta\in\Omega^{1}(M_{LR}), and therefore

[Γ​(ker⁡νR),Γ​(ker⁡νR)]νL⊂Γ​(ker⁡νR).\displaystyle\left[\Gamma(\ker\nu_{R}),\Gamma(\ker\nu_{R})\right]^{\nu_{L}}\subset\Gamma(\ker\nu_{R}).

This implies that, for all ξ,η∈Γ​(ker⁡νR)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(\ker\nu_{R}),

[νL​(ξ),νL​(η)]=νL​[ξ,η]νL,\displaystyle[\nu_{L}(\xi),\nu_{L}(\eta)]=\nu_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\nu_{L}},

and so νL​(ker⁡νR)\nu_{L}(\ker\nu_{R}) is involutive. By symmetry, also νR​(ker⁡νL)\nu_{R}(\ker\nu_{L}) is involutive. Again by (14), if ξ∈Γ​(ker⁡νR)\xi\in\Gamma(\ker\nu_{R}) and η∈Γ​(ker⁡νL)\eta\in\Gamma(\ker\nu_{L}), then

[ξ,η]νL∈Γ​(ker⁡νL),\displaystyle[\xi,\eta]^{\nu_{L}}\in\Gamma(\ker\nu_{L}), [ξ,η]νR∈Γ​(ker⁡νR),\displaystyle[\xi,\eta]^{\nu_{R}}\in\Gamma(\ker\nu_{R}),

and so

[νL​(ξ),νR​(η)]=νL​[ξ,η]νR+νR​[ξ,η]νL\displaystyle[\nu_{L}(\xi),\nu_{R}(\eta)]=\nu_{L}[\xi,\eta]^{\nu_{R}}+\nu_{R}[\xi,\eta]^{\nu_{L}}

lies in νL​(ker⁡(νR))+νR​(ker⁡(νL))\nu_{L}(\ker(\nu_{R}))+\nu_{R}(\ker(\nu_{L})). This concludes the proof.

∎

Corollary 37.

Let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} be weakly concurring Dirac structures on MM, and suppose there are surjective submersions

such that

ker⁡pL∗=ℳ​(L,R)∩T​M,\displaystyle\ker{p_{L}}_{*}=\mathscr{M}(L,R)\cap TM, ker⁡pR∗=ℳ​(R,L)∩T​M,\displaystyle\ker{p_{R}}_{*}=\mathscr{M}(R,L)\cap TM,
ker⁡qL∗=(ℳ​(L,R)⊛ℳ​(R,L))∩T​Mℳ​(L,R)∩T​M,\displaystyle\ker{q_{L}}_{*}=\frac{(\mathscr{M}(L,R)\circledast\mathscr{M}(R,L))\cap TM}{\mathscr{M}(L,R)\cap TM}, ker⁡qR∗=(ℳ​(L,R)⊛ℳ​(R,L))∩T​Mℳ​(R,L)∩T​M.\displaystyle\ker{q_{R}}_{*}=\frac{(\mathscr{M}(L,R)\circledast\mathscr{M}(R,L))\cap TM}{\mathscr{M}(R,L)\cap TM}.

Then LL, RR push forward, under each of these submersions, to weakly concurring Dirac structures, and

pL!​(L),\displaystyle{p_{L}}_{!}(L), pR!​(R),\displaystyle{p_{R}}_{!}(R), r!​(L),\displaystyle{r}_{!}(L), r!​(R)\displaystyle{r}_{!}(R)

are Poisson.

Example 38.

Consider the Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} on M=ℝ5​(x1,x2,x3,x4,x5)M=\mathbb{R}^{5}(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},x_{5}) of Example 32. Observe that

𝒩​(LM,RM)=⟨∂∂x1,∂∂x2+∂∂x4+d​x5,∂∂x3,d​x2−d​x4,∂∂x5−d​x4⟩\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(L_{M},R_{M})=\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}+\mathrm{d}x_{5},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}},\mathrm{d}x_{2}-\mathrm{d}x_{4},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}}-\mathrm{d}x_{4}\rangle

has kernel EL=⟨∂∂x1,∂∂x3⟩E_{L}=\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\rangle. Likewise, ER=⟨∂∂x3,∂∂x5⟩E_{R}=\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}}\rangle. We thus have a diamond

which induce the Dirac structures

LMEL=Gr​(∂∂x5∧∂∂x4),\displaystyle L_{M_{E_{L}}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}), RMEL=Gr​(∂∂x5)\displaystyle R_{M_{E_{L}}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}})
LMER=Gr​(∂∂x1),\displaystyle L_{M_{E_{R}}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}), RMER=Gr​(∂∂x2∧∂∂x1)\displaystyle R_{M_{E_{R}}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}})
LMEL​R=T∗​MEL​R,\displaystyle L_{M_{E_{LR}}}=T^{*}M_{E_{LR}}, RMEL​R=T∗​MEL​R.\displaystyle R_{M_{E_{LR}}}=T^{*}M_{E_{LR}}.

10. Further comments and examples

10.1. Moment map condition

The classical example of witnesses arises in Hamiltonian spaces [4, Example B]. We recall that setting and illustrate some of our constructions in this case.

Let a Lie group GG act on a manifold MM, and let LML_{M} be a Dirac structure on MM. We say that the action is Dirac if GG acts by Dirac automorphisms: for each g∈Gg\in G, the induced automorphism

g∗:𝕋​M→∼𝕋​M,\displaystyle g_{*}:\mathbb{T}M\xrightarrow{\raisebox{-1.59442pt}[0.0pt][0.0pt]{\smash{$\sim$}}}\mathbb{T}M, g∗​(u+α)=g∗​(u)+(g−1)∗​(α)\displaystyle g_{*}(u+\alpha)=g_{*}(u)+(g^{-1})^{*}(\alpha)

maps LML_{M} to itself. If GG is connected, the condition that GG act by Dirac automorphisms is equivalent to the requirement that

(15) [a​(v),Γ​(LM)]⊂Γ​(LM),\displaystyle[\mathrm{a}(v),\Gamma(L_{M})]\subset\Gamma(L_{M}), v∈𝔤,\displaystyle v\in\mathfrak{g},

where a:𝔤→𝔛​(M)\mathrm{a}:\mathfrak{g}\to\mathfrak{X}(M) stands for the infinitesimal action.

A Dirac action G↷(M,LM)G\curvearrowright(M,L_{M}) is Hamiltonian if it is equipped with an Ad∗{\rm Ad}^{*}-equivariant map

μ:M→𝔤∗,\displaystyle\mu:M\to\mathfrak{g}^{*}, μ​(g⋅x)=Adg−1∗​μ​(x),\displaystyle\mu(g\cdot x)=\mathrm{Ad}^{*}_{g^{-1}}\mu(x),

with the property that

(16) a​(v)+d​⟨μ,v⟩∈Γ​(LM),v∈𝔤.\displaystyle\mathrm{a}(v)+\mathrm{d}\langle\mu,v\rangle\ \in\Gamma(L_{M}),\quad v\in\mathfrak{g}.

We refer to G↷(M,LM)⟶μ𝔤∗G\curvearrowright(M,L_{M})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mu}}{{\longrightarrow}}\mathfrak{g}^{*} as a Hamiltonian space [22].

Definition 39.

Let G↷(M,LM)⟶μ𝔤∗G\curvearrowright(M,L_{M})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mu}}{{\longrightarrow}}\mathfrak{g}^{*} be a Hamiltonian space in which the action is free. We will call an element α∈𝔤∗\alpha\in\mathfrak{g}^{*} good if:

  1. a)

    μ\mu meets α\alpha cleanly — that is, X=μ−1​(α)X=\mu^{-1}(\alpha) is a smooth submanifold, and

    Tx​X={u∈Tx​M|μ∗​(u)=0},\displaystyle T_{x}X=\{u\in T_{x}M\ |\ \mu_{*}(u)=0\}, x∈X;\displaystyle x\in X;
  2. b)

    The isotropy subgroup Gα={g∈G|Adg−1∗​(α)=α}G_{\alpha}=\{g\in G\ |\ \mathrm{Ad}^{*}_{g^{-1}}(\alpha)=\alpha\} acts properly on XX;

  3. c)

    The map j:LM|X→𝔤∗j:L_{M}|_{X}\to\mathfrak{g}^{*} given by

    ⟨j​(u+ξ),v⟩:=⟨ξ,a​(v)⟩,\displaystyle\langle j(u+\xi),v\rangle:=\langle\xi,\mathrm{a}(v)\rangle, v∈𝔤\displaystyle v\in\mathfrak{g}

    has locally constant rank.

Lemma 40.

If α∈𝔤∗\alpha\in\mathfrak{g}^{*} is a good value of a free Hamiltonian space

G↷(M,LM)⟶μ𝔤∗,\displaystyle G\curvearrowright(M,L_{M})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mu}}{{\longrightarrow}}\mathfrak{g}^{*},

then EG⊂T​M|XE_{G}\subset TM|_{X} is a witness for LML_{M}, where

X=μ−1​(α),\displaystyle X=\mu^{-1}(\alpha), EG={a​(v)x|v∈𝔤,x∈X}.\displaystyle E_{G}=\{\mathrm{a}(v)_{x}\ |\ v\in\mathfrak{g},\ x\in X\}.
Proof.

The action being free on MM implies that ax:𝔤→Tx​M\mathrm{a}_{x}:\mathfrak{g}\to T_{x}M is injective for each x∈Mx\in M; therefore EG=a​(𝔤)|XE_{G}=\mathrm{a}(\mathfrak{g})|_{X} is a vector subbundle of T​M|XTM|_{X}, and this is an adapted subbundle because

EG∩T​X\displaystyle E_{G}\cap TX ={a​(v)|μ∗​a​(v)=π𝔤♯​(v)=0}=a​(𝔤α)|X.\displaystyle=\{\mathrm{a}(v)\ |\ \mu_{*}\mathrm{a}(v)=\pi_{\mathfrak{g}}^{\sharp}(v)=0\}=\mathrm{a}(\mathfrak{g}_{\alpha})|_{X}.

Note that

LM∩EG⟂\displaystyle L_{M}\cap E_{G}^{\perp} ={u+ξ∈LM|⟨ξ,a​(v)⟩=0,v∈𝔤}\displaystyle=\{u+\xi\in L_{M}\ |\ \langle\xi,\mathrm{a}(v)\rangle=0,\ v\in\mathfrak{g}\}
={u+ξ∈LM|⟨u,μ∗​(v)⟩=0,v∈𝔤}\displaystyle=\{u+\xi\in L_{M}\ |\ \langle u,\mu^{*}(v)\rangle=0,\ v\in\mathfrak{g}\}
=LM∩(N∗​X)⟂,\displaystyle=L_{M}\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp},

where in the second equality we used that a​(v)+μ∗​(v)∈LM\mathrm{a}(v)+\mu^{*}(v)\in L_{M} implies that

⟨ξ,a​(v)⟩+⟨u,μ∗​(v)⟩=⟨u+ξ,a​(v)+μ∗​(v)⟩=0\displaystyle\langle\xi,\mathrm{a}(v)\rangle+\langle u,\mu^{*}(v)\rangle=\langle u+\xi,\mathrm{a}(v)+\mu^{*}(v)\rangle=0

for all u+ξ∈LMu+\xi\in L_{M}. Condition Wit3) follows immediately, and also condition Wit1), since

LM∩I⟂\displaystyle L_{M}\cap I^{\perp} =(LM∩(N∗​X)⟂)∩F⟂=(LM∩E⟂)∩F⟂=LM∩E⟂,\displaystyle=\left(L_{M}\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp}\right)\cap F^{\perp}=\left(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}\right)\cap F^{\perp}=L_{M}\cap E^{\perp},

(and this implies that also LM∩IL_{M}\cap I and LM​[I]=LM∩I⟂+IL_{M}[I]=L_{M}\cap I^{\perp}+I are vector bundles.)

On the other hand, note that FF is the foliation by orbits of GαG_{\alpha}, and that if u+ξ∈Γ​(LM∩EG⟂)u+\xi\in\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E_{G}^{\perp}) and v∈𝔤αv\in\mathfrak{g}_{\alpha}, then

[a​(v),u+ξ]\displaystyle[\mathrm{a}(v),u+\xi] =[a​(v),u]+[a​(v),ξ]\displaystyle=[\mathrm{a}(v),u]+[\mathrm{a}(v),\xi]

again lies in Γ​(LM∩EG⟂)\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E_{G}^{\perp}), since the action is Dirac and

⟨[a​(v),ξ],a​(w)⟩=ℒa​(v)​⟨ξ,a​(w)⟩−⟨ξ,a​([v,w])⟩=0\displaystyle\langle[\mathrm{a}(v),\xi],\mathrm{a}(w)\rangle=\mathscr{L}_{\mathrm{a}(v)}\langle\xi,\mathrm{a}(w)\rangle-\langle\xi,\mathrm{a}([v,w])\rangle=0

for all w∈𝔤αw\in\mathfrak{g}_{\alpha} — that is,

(17) [Γ​(F),Γ​(LM∩E⟂)]⊂Γ​(F)+Γ​(LM∩E⟂).\displaystyle\left[\Gamma(F),\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp})\right]\subset\Gamma(F)+\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}).

Now, because

LM​[E]∩(N∗​X)⟂=(LM∩E⟂+E)∩(N∗​X)⟂=LM∩E⟂+F,\displaystyle L_{M}[E]\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp}=\left(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}+E\right)\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp}=L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}+F,

and

[Γ​(LM∩E⟂),Γ​(LM∩E⟂)]\displaystyle\left[\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}),\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp})\right] =[Γ​(LM∩(N∗​X)⟂),Γ​(LM∩(N∗​X)⟂)]\displaystyle=\left[\Gamma(L_{M}\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp}),\Gamma(L_{M}\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp})\right]
=Γ​(LM∩(N∗​X)⟂)=Γ​(LM∩E⟂)\displaystyle=\Gamma(L_{M}\cap(N^{*}X)^{\perp})=\Gamma(L_{M}\cap E^{\perp})

because the bracket of any two vector fields of MM which are tangent to XX is again tangent to XX, we deduce that Wit2) is satisfied. Hence EGE_{G} is a witness for LML_{M}. ∎

Remark 41.

Stronger versions of conditions a) and c) above are ensured by the notion of regularity introduced in [22], where one requires that α∈𝔤∗\alpha\in\mathfrak{g}^{*} be a regular value for the restriction of the momentum map μ|S:S→𝔤∗\mu|_{S}:S\to\mathfrak{g}^{*} to every leaf SS of LML_{M}. This condition in fact implies that μ\mu meets α\alpha transversely, and that j:LM|X→𝔤∗j:L_{M}|_{X}\rightarrow\mathfrak{g}^{*} is fiberwise surjective.

Remark 42.

In the setting of Poisson manifolds, a Hamiltonian GG-space whose infinitesimal action is free is necessarily regular in the sense of Remark 41. For general Dirac structures, this is no longer the case, as the following example illustrates.

Example 43.

Consider M=ℝ4M=\mathbb{R}^{4}, equipped with the action

G:=ℝ2↷M,(a,b)⋅(x1,x2,x3,x4):=(x1+a,x2,x3+a,x4+b)\displaystyle G:=\mathbb{R}^{2}\curvearrowright M,(a,b)\cdot(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4}):=(x_{1}+a,x_{2},x_{3}+a,x_{4}+b)

and the Dirac structure LML_{M} corresponding to the graph of the closed two-form ωM=d​x1∧d​x2\omega_{M}=\mathrm{d}x_{1}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{2}. Then

G↷(M,LM)⟶μ𝔤∗≃ℝ2,\displaystyle G\curvearrowright(M,L_{M})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mu}}{{\longrightarrow}}\mathfrak{g}^{*}\simeq\mathbb{R}^{2}, μ​(x1,x2,x3,x4)=(x2,0)\displaystyle\mu(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4})=(x_{2},0)

is a Hamiltonian GG-space. Let X:=μ−1​(0)X:=\mu^{-1}(0), and note that 0∈𝔤∗0\in\mathfrak{g}^{*} is a good value which is not regular. Thus

EG|X=EG∩T​X=⟨∂∂x1+∂∂x3,∂∂x4⟩\displaystyle E_{G}|_{X}=E_{G}\cap TX=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}+\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}\right\rangle

is a witness of the reduction of i!​(LM)=T​Xi^{!}(L_{M})=TX to the tangent bundle of a line.

10.2. Dirac-Nijenhuis structures

An endomorphism NM:T​M→T​MN_{M}:TM\to TM (i.e. a linear map covering the identity) gives rise to an NMN_{M}-twisted bracket [⋅,⋅]NM:𝔛​(M)×𝔛​(M)→𝔛​(M)[\cdot,\cdot]^{N_{M}}:\mathfrak{X}(M)\times\mathfrak{X}(M)\to\mathfrak{X}(M), given by

[u,v]NM:=[NM​(u),v]+[u,NM​(v)]−NM​[u,v].\displaystyle[u,v]^{N_{M}}:=[N_{M}(u),v]+[u,N_{M}(v)]-N_{M}[u,v].

We say that NMN_{M} is Nijenhuis if

NM​[u,v]NM=[NM​(u),NM​(v)],\displaystyle N_{M}[u,v]^{N_{M}}=[N_{M}(u),N_{M}(v)], u,v∈𝔛​(M).\displaystyle u,v\in\mathfrak{X}(M).

A smooth map ϕ:M→P\phi:M\to P relates two Nijenhuis maps NM:T​M→T​MN_{M}:TM\to TM and NP:T​P→T​PN_{P}:TP\to TP if

uM∈𝔛​(M),\displaystyle u_{M}\in\mathfrak{X}(M), uP∈𝔛​(P),\displaystyle u_{P}\in\mathfrak{X}(P), uM∼ϕuP\displaystyle u_{M}\sim_{\phi}u_{P}

implies that NM​(uM)∼ϕNP​(uP)N_{M}(u_{M})\sim_{\phi}N_{P}(u_{P}).

If LML_{M} is a Dirac structure on MM, we say that (LM,NM)(L_{M},N_{M}) is Dirac-Nijenhuis [28, Section 3.3] if

(NM,NM∗)​(L)⊂L,\displaystyle(N_{M},N_{M}^{*})(L)\subset L, {Γ​(𝕋​M),Γ​(LM)}NM⊂Γ​(LM),\displaystyle\{\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M),\Gamma(L_{M})\}_{N_{M}}\subset\Gamma(L_{M}),

where {⋅,⋅}NM:Γ​(𝕋​M)×Γ​(𝕋​M)→Γ​(𝕋​M)\{\cdot,\cdot\}_{N_{M}}:\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M)\times\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M)\to\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M) denotes

(18) {u+ξ,v+η}NM\displaystyle\{u+\xi,v+\eta\}_{N_{M}} =[v,NM​(u)]−NM​[v,u]+[η,NM​(u)]−[NM∗​(η),u].\displaystyle=[v,N_{M}(u)]-N_{M}[v,u]+[\eta,N_{M}(u)]-[N_{M}^{*}(\eta),u].
Example 44.

When LML_{M} is the graph of a Poisson structure πM\pi_{M}, the condition that (LM,NM)(L_{M},N_{M}) be Dirac-Nijenhuis is equivalent to

NM​πM=πM​NM∗,\displaystyle N_{M}\pi_{M}=\pi_{M}N_{M}^{*}, NM∗​[ξ,η]πM=[NM∗​(ξ),πM​(η)]+[πM​(ξ),NM∗​(η)]\displaystyle N_{M}^{*}[\xi,\eta]^{\pi_{M}}=[N_{M}^{*}(\xi),\pi_{M}(\eta)]+[\pi_{M}(\xi),N_{M}^{*}(\eta)]

for all ξ,η∈Ω1​(M)\xi,\eta\in\Omega^{1}(M) — that is, that (πM,NM)(\pi_{M},N_{M}) be a P​NPN-structure.

Example 45.

When LML_{M} is the graph of a closed two-form ωM\omega_{M}, the condition that (LM,NM)(L_{M},N_{M}) be Dirac-Nijenhuis is equivalent to

ω​NM=NM∗​ω,\displaystyle\omega N_{M}=N_{M}^{*}\omega, d​(ω​NM)=0;\displaystyle\mathrm{d}(\omega N_{M})=0;

that is, that (ωM,NM)(\omega_{M},N_{M}) be a Ω​N\Omega N-structure.

Lemma 46.

Assume that the diagram (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) relates Nijenhuis endomorphisms444As explained in [8, Section 3] (see also [10, Theorem 2.1]), the reducibility of the Nijenhuis map is independent of that of the Dirac structures. Observe also that the proof of the analogous result for the weak Dirac-Nijenhuis structures of [34] is automatic.

NM:T​M→T​M,\displaystyle N_{M}:TM\to TM, NX:T​X→T​X,\displaystyle N_{X}:TX\to TX, NY:T​Y→T​Y.\displaystyle N_{Y}:TY\to TY.

If (LM,NM)(L_{M},N_{M}) is a Dirac-Nijenhuis structure, and LML_{M} has a Dirac reduction to LYL_{Y} on YY, then also (LY,NY)(L_{Y},N_{Y}) is a Dirac-Nijenhuis structure.

Proof.

Because i!​(LM)i^{!}(L_{M}) is smooth in an open, dense set U⊂XU\subset X, the result follows by density from juxtaposing [28, Proposition 4.1] and [28, Proposition 4.4]. ∎

A Dirac-Nijenhuis structure (LM,NM)(L_{M},N_{M}) gives rise to Lagrangian families

𝔏NMn​(LM)\displaystyle\mathfrak{L}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M}) =(NMn,id)​(LM)+LM∩T​M,\displaystyle=(N_{M}^{n},\mathrm{id})(L_{M})+L_{M}\cap TM,
ℜNMn​(LM)\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M}) =(id,(NM∗)n)​(LM)+LM∩T∗​M\displaystyle=(\mathrm{id},(N_{M}^{*})^{n})(L_{M})+L_{M}\cap T^{*}M

of left- and right shifts (see [34, Section 9] and [28, Proposition 4.8]).

Example 47.

If (πM,NM)(\pi_{M},N_{M}) is a P​NPN-structure, then

𝔏NMi​Gr​(πM)=Gr​(NMi​πM).\displaystyle\mathfrak{L}_{N_{M}^{i}}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})=\mathrm{Gr}(N_{M}^{i}\pi_{M}).

If (ωM,NM)(\omega_{M},N_{M}) is an Ω​N\Omega N-structure, then

ℜNMi​Gr​(ωM)=Gr​(ωM​NMi).\displaystyle\mathfrak{R}_{N_{M}^{i}}\mathrm{Gr}(\omega_{M})=\mathrm{Gr}(\omega_{M}N_{M}^{i}).
Corollary 48.

In the setting of Lemma 46, the shifts

(𝔏NMn​(LM),NM),\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{L}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M}),N_{M}\right), (ℜNMn​(LM),NM),\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{R}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M}),N_{M}\right),

reduce to Dirac-Nijenhuis structures

(𝔏NYn​(LY),NY),\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{L}_{N_{Y}^{n}}(L_{Y}),N_{Y}\right), (ℜNYn​(LY),NY),\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{R}_{N_{Y}^{n}}(L_{Y}),N_{Y}\right),

wherever the latter are smooth. Moreover, any two such shifts concur weakly.

Proof.

As explained in [34, Section 9], the left- and right shifts of Dirac-Nijenhuis structures are always involutive, as are the tangent and cotangent products of any two such shifts. Moreover,

(𝔏NMn​(LM),NM),\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{L}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M}),N_{M}\right), (ℜNMn​(LM),NM)\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{R}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M}),N_{M}\right)

are Dirac-Nijenhuis structures wherever they are smooth. It follows from Lemma 46 that (LY,NY)(L_{Y},N_{Y}), and therefore

(𝔏NYn​(LY),NY),\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{L}_{N_{Y}^{n}}(L_{Y}),N_{Y}\right), (ℜNYn​(LY),NY),\displaystyle\left(\mathfrak{R}_{N_{Y}^{n}}(L_{Y}),N_{Y}\right),

are Dirac-Nijenhuis wherever they are smooth, and since

p!​i!​(LM)=LY,\displaystyle p_{!}i^{!}(L_{M})=L_{Y}, NX∼iNM,\displaystyle N_{X}\sim_{i}N_{M}, NX∼pNY,\displaystyle N_{X}\sim_{p}N_{Y},

we have that

p!​i!​𝔏NMn​(LM)=𝔏NYn​(LY),\displaystyle p_{!}i^{!}\mathfrak{L}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M})=\mathfrak{L}_{N_{Y}^{n}}(L_{Y}), p!​i!​ℜNMn​(LM)=ℜNYn​(LY).\displaystyle p_{!}i^{!}\mathfrak{R}_{N_{M}^{n}}(L_{M})=\mathfrak{R}_{N_{Y}^{n}}(L_{Y}).

∎

Example 49.

Let α∈𝔤∗\alpha\in\mathfrak{g}^{*} be a good value of a free Hamiltonian space

G↷(M,Gr​(π0))⟶μ𝔤∗,\displaystyle G\curvearrowright(M,\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{0}))\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mu}}{{\longrightarrow}}\mathfrak{g}^{*},

where π0\pi_{0} is a symplectic Poisson structure, the inverse of a closed two-form ω0∈Ω2​(M)\omega_{0}\in\Omega^{2}(M). Suppose ω1\omega_{1} is a GαG_{\alpha}-invariant, closed two-form, such that N:=π0♯​ω1♯:T​M→T​MN:=\pi_{0}^{\sharp}\omega_{1}^{\sharp}:TM\to TM is a Nijenhuis endomorphism which satisfies

N−1​(T​X)=T​X,\displaystyle N^{-1}(TX)=TX, X:=μ−1​(α).\displaystyle X:=\mu^{-1}(\alpha).

Then

  1. a)

    (ω0,N)(\omega_{0},N) is a Ω​N\Omega N-structure,

  2. b)

    (π0,N)(\pi_{0},N) is a P​NPN-structure,

  3. c)

    EG⊂T​M|XE_{G}\subset TM|_{X} is a witness for (the graphs of)

    ωn:=ω0​Nn,\displaystyle\omega_{n}:=\omega_{0}N^{n}, πn:=Nn​π0,\displaystyle\pi_{n}:=N^{n}\pi_{0},

    where

    EG={a​(v)x|v∈𝔤,x∈X}.\displaystyle E_{G}=\{\mathrm{a}(v)_{x}\ |\ v\in\mathfrak{g},\ x\in X\}.

Indeed, by [32, Proposition 20], Gr​(π0)\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{0}) and Gr​(ω1)\mathrm{Gr}(\omega_{1}) concur iff

ω1​[u,v]π0​ω1=[ω1​(u),ω1​(v)]π0,\displaystyle\omega_{1}[u,v]^{\pi_{0}\omega_{1}}=[\omega_{1}(u),\omega_{1}(v)]^{\pi_{0}}, u,v∈𝔛​(M).\displaystyle u,v\in\mathfrak{X}(M).

Because π0\pi_{0} is symplectic, this happens exactly when N=π0​ω1N=\pi_{0}\omega_{1} is Nijenhuis:

N​[u,v]N=π0​ω1​[u,v]π0​ω1=π0​[ω1​(u),ω1​(v)]π0=[π0​ω1​(u),π0​ω1​(v)]=[N​(u),N​(v)].\displaystyle N[u,v]^{N}=\pi_{0}\omega_{1}[u,v]^{\pi_{0}\omega_{1}}=\pi_{0}[\omega_{1}(u),\omega_{1}(v)]^{\pi_{0}}=[\pi_{0}\omega_{1}(u),\pi_{0}\omega_{1}(v)]=[N(u),N(v)].

Thus (ω0,N)(\omega_{0},N) is an Ω​N\Omega N-structure, and therefore also (π0,N)(\pi_{0},N) is a P​NPN-structure. By the moment map condition (Lemma 40), EGE_{G} is a witness for Gr​(π0)=Gr​(ω0)\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{0})=\mathrm{Gr}(\omega_{0}). Because we assume that N−1​(T​X)=T​XN^{-1}(TX)=TX, any Dirac structure RR of the form

R=Gr​(Ni​π0),\displaystyle R=\mathrm{Gr}(N^{i}\pi_{0}), or R=Gr​(ω0​Nj)\displaystyle R=\mathrm{Gr}(\omega_{0}N^{j})

satisfies

(19) R∩EG⟂=R∩N∗​X⟂,\displaystyle R\cap E_{G}^{\perp}=R\cap N^{*}X^{\perp},

and because πi:=Ni​π0\pi_{i}:=N^{i}\pi_{0} and ωj:=ω0​Nj\omega_{j}:=\omega_{0}N^{j} are GαG_{\alpha}-invariant, we also have that

(20) [Γ​(F),Γ​(R∩E⟂)]⊂Γ​(F)+Γ​(R∩E⟂).\displaystyle\left[\Gamma(F),\Gamma(R\cap E^{\perp})\right]\subset\Gamma(F)+\Gamma(R\cap E^{\perp}).

As in the proof of Lemma 40, (19) and (20) imply that EGE_{G} is a witness for RR.

Corollary 50.

Let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} be weakly concurring Dirac structures on MM, and suppose

is the diagram of either Corollary 31 or Corollary 37. If NM:T​M→T​MN_{M}:TM\to TM is a Nijenhuis endomorphism, and

(LM,NM),\displaystyle(L_{M},N_{M}), (RM,NM)\displaystyle(R_{M},N_{M})

are Dirac-Nijenhuis, then all vertices in the diagram above inherit Nijenhuis structures

NML:T​ML→T​ML,\displaystyle N_{M_{L}}:TM_{L}\to TM_{L}, NMR:T​MR→T​MR,\displaystyle N_{M_{R}}:TM_{R}\to TM_{R}, NML​R:T​ML​R→T​ML​R,\displaystyle N_{M_{LR}}:TM_{LR}\to TM_{LR},

which are related by the diagram,

NM∼pLNML,\displaystyle N_{M}\sim_{p_{L}}N_{M_{L}}, NM∼pRNMR,\displaystyle N_{M}\sim_{p_{R}}N_{M_{R}}, NML∼qRNML​R,\displaystyle N_{M_{L}}\sim_{q_{R}}N_{M_{LR}}, NMR∼qLNML​R,\displaystyle N_{M_{R}}\sim_{q_{L}}N_{M_{LR}},

and the induced Dirac structures on the vertices are Dirac-Nijenhuis for the corresponding endomorphism.

Proof.

In the context of Corollary 31, (LM,NM)(L_{M},N_{M}) Dirac-Nijenhuis implies that

(Gr​(K​(LM)),NM)\displaystyle(\mathrm{Gr}(K(L_{M})),N_{M})

is Dirac-Nijenhuis by [28, Proposition 3.13], and therefore NML:T​ML→T​MLN_{M_{L}}:TM_{L}\to TM_{L} exists as in [28, Corollary 4.5]. Similarly, a Nijenhuis endomorphism NMR:T​MR→T​MRN_{M_{R}}:TM_{R}\to TM_{R} exists. As explained in [34, Section 9],

(LM⊛RM,NM)\displaystyle(L_{M}\circledast R_{M},N_{M})

is Dirac-Nijenhuis wherever smooth, and therefore the graph of

K=K​(LM⊛RM)\displaystyle K=K(L_{M}\circledast R_{M})

is Dirac-Nijenhuis for NMN_{M}. Therefore also a Nijenhuis endomorphism

NML​R:T​ML​R→T​ML​R\displaystyle N_{M_{LR}}:TM_{LR}\to TM_{LR}

exists. Thus the Dirac structures induced on MLM_{L},MRM_{R} and ML​RM_{LR} are Dirac-Nijenhuis for the corresponding Nijenhuis endomorphisms.

In the context of Corollary 37, we observe as in [34] that (18) is compatible with Dirac products, in the following sense: whenever xL,yL∈Γ​(LM)x_{L},y_{L}\in\Gamma(L_{M}) and xR,yR∈Γ​(RM)x_{R},y_{R}\in\Gamma(R_{M}) are such that

prT​(xL)=prT​(xR),\displaystyle\mathrm{pr}_{T}(x_{L})=\mathrm{pr}_{T}(x_{R}), prT∗​(yL)=prT∗​(yR),\displaystyle\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(y_{L})=\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(y_{R}),

we write

xL⋆xR=xL+prT∗​(xR),\displaystyle x_{L}\star x_{R}=x_{L}+\mathrm{pr}_{T^{*}}(x_{R}), yL⊛yR=yL+prT​(yR).\displaystyle y_{L}\circledast y_{R}=y_{L}+\mathrm{pr}_{T}(y_{R}).

Then

{z,xL⋆xR}NM\displaystyle\{z,x_{L}\star x_{R}\}_{N_{M}} ={z,xL}NM⋆{z,xR}NM,\displaystyle=\{z,x_{L}\}_{N_{M}}\star\{z,x_{R}\}_{N_{M}},
{z,yL⊛yR}NM\displaystyle\{z,y_{L}\circledast y_{R}\}_{N_{M}} ={z,yL}NM⊛{z,yR}NM\displaystyle=\{z,y_{L}\}_{N_{M}}\circledast\{z,y_{R}\}_{N_{M}}

for all z∈Γ​(𝕋​M)z\in\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M). This implies that

𝒩​(LM,RM)=LM⋆(RM⊛ℛ−1​(LM)),\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(L_{M},R_{M})=L_{M}\star\left(R_{M}\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(L_{M})\right), 𝒩​(RM,LM)=RM⋆(LM⊛ℛ−1​(RM))\displaystyle\mathscr{N}(R_{M},L_{M})=R_{M}\star\left(L_{M}\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(R_{M})\right)

are Dirac-Nijenhuis for NMN_{M} (wherever smooth), and therefore also

ℳ​(LM,RM),\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(L_{M},R_{M}), ℳ​(RM,LM),\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(R_{M},L_{M}), ℳ​(LM,RM)⊛ℳ​(RM,LM)\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(L_{M},R_{M})\circledast\mathscr{M}(R_{M},L_{M})

are Dirac-Nijenhuis for NMN_{M} (wherever smooth). This implies that Nijenhuis endomorphisms

NML:T​ML→T​ML,\displaystyle N_{M_{L}}:TM_{L}\to TM_{L}, NMR:T​MR→T​MR,\displaystyle N_{M_{R}}:TM_{R}\to TM_{R}, NML​R:T​ML​R→T​ML​R\displaystyle N_{M_{LR}}:TM_{LR}\to TM_{LR}

exist, related by the submersions in the diagram, and all the Dirac structures induced on MLM_{L},MRM_{R} and ML​RM_{LR} are Dirac-Nijenhuis for the corresponding Nijenhuis endomorphisms. ∎

10.3. Complex Dirac structures

There is a version of Dirac geometry with complex coefficients, which goes as follows: the scalar extensions of the symmetric bilinear form and of the Dorfman bracket

(21) ⟨⋅,⋅⟩:𝕋​M×𝕋​M→ℝ,\displaystyle\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:\mathbb{T}M\times\mathbb{T}M\to\mathbb{R}, [⋅,⋅]:Γ​(𝕋​M)×Γ​(𝕋​M)→Γ​(𝕋​M)\displaystyle[\cdot,\cdot]:\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M)\times\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M)\to\Gamma(\mathbb{T}M)

give rise to

⟨⋅,⋅⟩ℂ:𝕋ℂ​M×𝕋ℂ​M→ℂ,\displaystyle\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathbb{C}}:\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M\times\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M\to\mathbb{C}, [⋅,⋅]ℂ:Γ​(𝕋ℂ​M)×Γ​(𝕋ℂ​M)→Γ​(𝕋ℂ​M),\displaystyle[\cdot,\cdot]_{\mathbb{C}}:\Gamma(\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M)\times\Gamma(\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M)\to\Gamma(\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M),

where 𝕋ℂ​M:=𝕋​M⊗ℂ\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M:=\mathbb{T}M\otimes\mathbb{C} stands for the complex generalized tangent bundle of MM. A ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure LML_{M} is a complex subbundle LM⊂𝕋ℂ​ML_{M}\subset\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M which is Lagrangian and involutive:

LM=LM⟂,\displaystyle L_{M}=L_{M}^{\perp}, ⟨[Γ​(LM),Γ​(LM)]ℂ,Γ​(LM)⟩ℂ=0.\displaystyle\left\langle\left[\Gamma(L_{M}),\Gamma(L_{M})\right]_{\mathbb{C}},\Gamma(L_{M})\right\rangle_{\mathbb{C}}=0.
Example 51.

If LM⊂𝕋​ML_{M}\subset\mathbb{T}M is a Dirac structure on MM, its scalar extension LM⊗ℂL_{M}\otimes\mathbb{C} is a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure on MM. If LM⊂𝕋ℂ​ML_{M}\subset\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M is a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure on MM, then so is its conjugate

LM¯={u+ξ∈𝕋ℂ​M|u¯+ξ¯∈LM}.\displaystyle\overline{L_{M}}=\{u+\xi\in\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M\ |\ \overline{u}+\overline{\xi}\in L_{M}\}.

A ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure LML_{M} is the scalar extension of a Dirac structure if and only if it is real, LM=LM¯L_{M}=\overline{L_{M}}.

Example 52 (Building blocks).

In analogy to the real case, there are notable building blocks for ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structures on MM:

involutive structures:

for a complex vector subbundle E⊂Tℂ​ME\subset T_{\mathbb{C}}M, Gr​(E)=E⊕E∘\mathrm{Gr}(E)=E\oplus E^{\circ} is a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure exactly when Γ​(E)\Gamma(E) is involutive under (21);

closed complex forms:

the graph of ω=ω1+i​ω2\omega=\omega_{1}+i\omega_{2}, where ω1,ω2∈Ω2​(M)\omega_{1},\omega_{2}\in\Omega^{2}(M) is a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure exactly when ω\omega is closed; equivalently, if

ω♯​[u,v]ℂ=[ω♯​(u),v]ℂ+[u,ω♯​(v)]ℂ\displaystyle\omega^{\sharp}[u,v]_{\mathbb{C}}=[\omega^{\sharp}(u),v]_{\mathbb{C}}+[u,\omega^{\sharp}(v)]_{\mathbb{C}}

for all u,v∈Γ​(Tℂ​M)u,v\in\Gamma(T_{\mathbb{C}}M);

complex Poisson structures:

the graph of π=π1+i​π2\pi=\pi_{1}+i\pi_{2}, where π1,π2∈𝔛2​(M)\pi_{1},\pi_{2}\in\mathfrak{X}^{2}(M), is a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure exactly when

π♯​[ξ,η]ℂπ=[π♯​(ξ),π♯​(η)]ℂ\displaystyle\pi^{\sharp}[\xi,\eta]_{\mathbb{C}}^{\pi}=[\pi^{\sharp}(\xi),\pi^{\sharp}(\eta)]_{\mathbb{C}}

for all ξ,η∈Γ​(Tℂ∗​M)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(T^{*}_{\mathbb{C}}M), where

[ξ,η]ℂπ=[π♯​(ξ),η]ℂ+[ξ,π♯​(η)]ℂ\displaystyle[\xi,\eta]_{\mathbb{C}}^{\pi}=[\pi^{\sharp}(\xi),\eta]_{\mathbb{C}}+[\xi,\pi^{\sharp}(\eta)]_{\mathbb{C}}

is the Koszul bracket of π\pi.

Remark 53.

Formal similarities notwithstanding, ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structures are quite different from Dirac structures. That is mostly because involutive structures, like

E=ℂ​∂∂z⊂Tℂ​ℂ,\displaystyle E=\mathbb{C}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\subset T_{\mathbb{C}}\mathbb{C},

do not in general partition the ambient space into leaves, unless said involutive structure is real, E=E¯E=\overline{E}. See [16] for further details.

The operations of Dirac products and stretching by an isotropic family are defined for ℂ\mathbb{C}-Lagrangian families, as they are in the real case. We describe the complex versions of the real reduction schemes: fix as before a triangle

(𝔗\mathfrak{T}) M⟵iX⟶pY\displaystyle M\stackrel{{\scriptstyle i}}{{\longleftarrow}}X\stackrel{{\scriptstyle p}}{{\longrightarrow}}Y

A complex subbundle E⊂Tℂ​ME\subset T_{\mathbb{C}}M will be called adapted if

E∩Tℂ​X=Fℂ,\displaystyle E\cap T_{\mathbb{C}}X=F_{\mathbb{C}},

is the scalar extension of a foliation F⊂T​XF\subset TX, in which case we write

Iℂ=Fℂ⊕Nℂ∗​X⊂𝕋ℂ​M|X.\displaystyle I_{\mathbb{C}}=F_{\mathbb{C}}\oplus N^{*}_{\mathbb{C}}X\subset\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M|_{X}.

An adapted subbundle is a complex witness of a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure LM⊂𝕋ℂ​ML_{M}\subset\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M if:

CWit1) LM​[Iℂ]L_{M}[I_{\mathbb{C}}] is a smooth subbundle of 𝕋ℂ​M\mathbb{T}_{\mathbb{C}}M; CWit2) LM​[E]∩(Nℂ∗​X)⟂L_{M}[E]\cap(N_{\mathbb{C}}^{*}X)^{\perp} is involutive along ii; CWit3) LM∩E⟂⊂(Nℂ∗​X)⟂L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}\subset(N_{\mathbb{C}}^{*}X)^{\perp}.

With this notion of complex witness in place, we may state:

Theorem 54 (Complex reduction schemes).

Let LML_{M} be a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure on MM.

  1. a)

    LM​[Iℂ]L_{M}[I_{\mathbb{C}}] is ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac exactly when p!​i!​(LM)=LYp_{!}i^{!}(L_{M})=L_{Y} is a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure on YY;

  2. b)

    LM​[Iℂ]L_{M}[I_{\mathbb{C}}] is ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac if a complex witness EE exists for LML_{M};

  3. c)

    If EE is a complex witness for two weakly concurring ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M}, then also LYL_{Y} and RYR_{Y} concur weakly;

  4. d)

    If X=MX=M, EE is a complex witness for LML_{M} if and only if LM⊛Gr​(E)L_{M}\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(E) is ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac.

  5. e)

    If LM=Gr​(π)L_{M}=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi) corresponds to a complex Poisson structure, EE is a complex witness for LML_{M} if and only if E∘E^{\circ} is a Lie subalgebroid of (Tℂ∗​M,[⋅,⋅]π)(T^{*}_{\mathbb{C}}M,[\cdot,\cdot]^{\pi}).

Proof.

This essentially consists of the observation that, because E∩Tℂ​XE\cap T_{\mathbb{C}}X is the scalar extension of a foliation, none of the complications of ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structures (vis à vis usual Dirac structures) intervene, and the arguments of Sections 4 and 6 carry over to the case of complex witnesses. ∎

Example 55.

We illustrate how Magri’s original recipe can be derived elegantly using the formalism of ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structures. Let πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R} be commuting Poisson structures, and define

π∈Γ​(∧2Tℂ​M),\displaystyle\pi\in\Gamma(\wedge^{2}T_{\mathbb{C}}M), π=πL+i​πR.\displaystyle\pi=\pi_{L}+i\pi_{R}.

Because πL\pi_{L} and πR\pi_{R} are Poisson and commute, the following are concurring ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structures:

L=Gr​(π),\displaystyle L=\mathrm{Gr}(\pi), L¯=Gr​(π¯).\displaystyle\overline{L}=\mathrm{Gr}(\overline{\pi}).

Consider the complex Lagrangian family

L⋆L¯={π♯​(ξ)+ξ+η|ξ,η∈Tℂ∗​M,π♯​(ξ)=π¯♯​(η)}.\displaystyle L\star\overline{L}=\{\pi^{\sharp}(\xi)+\xi+\eta\ |\ \xi,\eta\in T^{*}_{\mathbb{C}}M,\ \pi^{\sharp}(\xi)=\overline{\pi}^{\sharp}(\eta)\}.

Then L⋆L¯L\star\overline{L} is involutive. Because

(L⋆L¯)∩Tℂ​M=π♯​(ker⁡(π♯+π¯♯))\displaystyle(L\star\overline{L})\cap T_{\mathbb{C}}M=\pi^{\sharp}(\ker(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp}))

is the complexification of E=πR♯​(ker⁡(πL♯))E=\pi_{R}^{\sharp}(\ker(\pi_{L}^{\sharp})) [31, Corollary 7.5], also EE is involutive; it is thus a foliation if EE has locally constant rank. This proves a). For b), suppose that the leaves of EE are the fibres of a surjective submersion p:M→Yp:M\to Y. Then a sufficient condition for W:=E⊗ℂW:=E\otimes\mathbb{C} to be a complex witness for LL is that its annihilator

W∘⊂(Tℂ∗​M,[⋅,⋅]π)\displaystyle W^{\circ}\subset(T^{*}_{\mathbb{C}}M,[\cdot,\cdot]^{\pi})

be a complex Lie subalgebroid of the cotangent Lie algebroid of π\pi. To see that this is so, note that

W∘=π♯​(ker⁡(π♯+π¯♯))∘=(π♯)−1​(im​(π♯+π¯♯)),\displaystyle W^{\circ}=\pi^{\sharp}(\ker(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp}))^{\circ}=(\pi^{\sharp})^{-1}(\mathrm{im}(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp})),

and suppose ξ,η∈Γ​(W∘)\xi,\eta\in\Gamma(W^{\circ}) are such that

π♯​(ξ)=(π♯+π¯♯)​(ξ′),\displaystyle\pi^{\sharp}(\xi)=(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp})(\xi^{\prime}), π♯​(η)=(π♯+π¯♯)​(η′),\displaystyle\pi^{\sharp}(\eta)=(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp})(\eta^{\prime}),

for some ξ′,η′∈Γ​(Tℂ∗​M)\xi^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}\in\Gamma(T^{*}_{\mathbb{C}}M). Then

π♯​[ξ,η]π=[π♯​(ξ),π♯​(η)]=[(π♯+π¯♯)​(ξ′),(π♯+π¯♯)​(η′)]=(π♯+π¯♯)​[ξ′,η′]π+π¯\displaystyle\pi^{\sharp}[\xi,\eta]^{\pi}=[\pi^{\sharp}(\xi),\pi^{\sharp}(\eta)]=[(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp})(\xi^{\prime}),(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp})(\eta^{\prime})]=(\pi^{\sharp}+\overline{\pi}^{\sharp})[\xi^{\prime},\eta^{\prime}]^{\pi+\overline{\pi}}

shows that [ξ,η]π∈Γ​(W∘)[\xi,\eta]^{\pi}\in\Gamma(W^{\circ}). By Theorem 54, p!​Gr​(π)p_{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi) is a ℂ\mathbb{C}-Dirac structure on YY, which is the graph of a complex Poisson structure ν=νL+i​νR\nu=\nu_{L}+i\nu_{R} on YY since

p!​Gr​(π)∩Tℂ​M\displaystyle p_{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi)\cap T_{\mathbb{C}}M ={p∗​(u)+ξ|u=π♯​(ξ),ξ=0}=0.\displaystyle=\{p_{*}(u)+\xi\ |\ u=\pi^{\sharp}(\xi),\ \xi=0\}=0.

Note finally that

p!​Gr​(πL)=Gr​(νL),\displaystyle p_{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{L})=\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{L}), p!​Gr​(πR)=Gr​(νR);\displaystyle p_{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{R})=\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{R});

hence EE is a witness for πL\pi_{L} and for πR\pi_{R}.

10.4. The full picture of Magri’s recipe

In Magri’s original recipe, rather than being performed globally, the reduction took place along a submanifold — namely, the leaf of one of the Poisson structures. An analogous statement holds for the Dirac version of Magri’s recipe, which generalizes the Poisson one. We state the “left” version only, the “right” one being obtained mutatis mutandis:

Proposition 56.

Let LML_{M} and RMR_{M} be weakly concurring Dirac structures, for which

K:=K​(LM⊛RM)=(LM⊛RM)∩T​M,\displaystyle K:=K(L_{M}\circledast R_{M})=(L_{M}\circledast R_{M})\cap TM, RM⊛Gr​(K)\displaystyle R_{M}\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(K)

are vector bundles. Let i:X→Mi:X\to M be a leaf of RM⊛Gr​(K)R_{M}\circledast\mathrm{Gr}(K), and denote by

ℳ​(LM,RM)=EL⊕EL∘\displaystyle\mathscr{M}(L_{M},R_{M})=E_{L}\oplus E_{L}^{\circ}

the Magri recipe of Definition 34. If EL∩T​XE_{L}\cap TX is a simple foliation, with leaf space p:X→Yp:X\to Y, and

E:=EL|X,\displaystyle E:=E_{L}|_{X}, LM∩E,\displaystyle L_{M}\cap E, RM∩E\displaystyle R_{M}\cap E

are smooth, then EE is simultaneously a witness for LML_{M} and for RMR_{M}.

Proof.

Observe first that

T​X=K|X+ΠR​(K∘).\displaystyle TX=K|_{X}+\Pi_{R}(K^{\circ}).

Retain the notation of the proof of Theorem 36, and recall that

EL∘={ξ∈K​(L)∘|ΠL​(ξ)∈ΠR​(K∘)+K}.\displaystyle E_{L}^{\circ}=\{\xi\in K(L)^{\circ}\ |\ \Pi_{L}(\xi)\in\Pi_{R}(K^{\circ})+K\}.

Because

LM∩EL⟂=K​(L)+ℛΠL​(EL∘),\displaystyle L_{M}\cap E_{L}^{\perp}=K(L)+\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{L}}(E_{L}^{\circ}), RM∩EL⟂=K​(R)+ℛΠR​(EL∘∩K∘)\displaystyle R_{M}\cap E_{L}^{\perp}=K(R)+\mathcal{R}_{\Pi_{R}}(E_{L}^{\circ}\cap K^{\circ})

by (8) and (11), we see that

LM∩E⟂⊂N∗​X⟂,\displaystyle L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}\subset N^{*}X^{\perp}, RM∩E⟂⊂N∗​X⟂.\displaystyle R_{M}\cap E^{\perp}\subset N^{*}X^{\perp}.

Thus EE satisfies Wit3) with respect to both LML_{M} and RMR_{M}, and this implies that

LM​[I]\displaystyle L_{M}[I] =LM​[E]∩N∗​X⟂+N∗​X=F+LM∩E⟂+N∗​X\displaystyle=L_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}X^{\perp}+N^{*}X=F+L_{M}\cap E^{\perp}+N^{*}X
RM​[I]\displaystyle R_{M}[I] =RM​[E]∩N∗​X⟂+N∗​X=F+RM∩E⟂+N∗​X.\displaystyle=R_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}X^{\perp}+N^{*}X=F+R_{M}\cap E^{\perp}+N^{*}X.

Because LM​[I]L_{M}[I] and RM​[I]R_{M}[I] are Lagrangian families, and FF, LM∩E⟂L_{M}\cap E^{\perp} and RM∩E⟂R_{M}\cap E^{\perp} are smooth by hypothesis, it follows that LM​[I]L_{M}[I] and RM​[I]R_{M}[I] are smooth, and so EE satisfies Wit1) with respect to both LML_{M} and RMR_{M}. Observe finally that, by Theorem 36, LM​[EL]L_{M}[E_{L}] and RM​[EL]R_{M}[E_{L}] are involutive, and therefore so are

LM​[E]∩N∗​V⟂=LM​[EL]∩N∗​V⟂,\displaystyle L_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}V^{\perp}=L_{M}[E_{L}]\cap N^{*}V^{\perp}, RM​[E]∩N∗​V⟂=RM​[EL]∩N∗​V⟂\displaystyle R_{M}[E]\cap N^{*}V^{\perp}=R_{M}[E_{L}]\cap N^{*}V^{\perp}

for all connected open set V⊂XV\subset X that ii embeds. Therefore EE also satisfies Wit2) with respect to LML_{M} and RMR_{M}. ∎

Remark 57.

In the setting of Proposition 56, suppose X⊂MX\subset M is any submanifold which is saturated by leaves of LML_{M} and of RMR_{M}. Then the Magri recipe can be performed along XX, under constant rank conditions similar to those of loc. cit..

Remark 58.

Suppose a surjective submersion with connected fibres r:M→Yr:M\to Y exists, whose vertical bundle is K=K​(L)+K​(R)K=K(L)+K(R). Then we have an equality

ℳ​(r!​r!​(L),r!​r!​(R))=r!​ℳ​(r!​(L),r!​(R))\displaystyle\mathscr{M}\left(r^{!}r_{!}(L),r^{!}r_{!}(R)\right)=r^{!}\mathscr{M}\left(r_{!}(L),r_{!}(R)\right)

Therefore the reduction under the Magri recipe (Theorem 36) of r!​r!​(L)r^{!}r_{!}(L) and r!​r!​(R)r^{!}r_{!}(R) coincides with the concatenation of the Reduction by kernels of Theorem 30, followed by Magri’s original recipe for the Poisson structures r!​(L)r_{!}(L) and r!​(R)r_{!}(R).

Example 59.

As shown in Example 32, the Dirac structures

LM=Gr​(d​x2∧d​x3+d​x4∧d​x5),\displaystyle L_{M}=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\mathrm{d}x_{2}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{3}+\mathrm{d}x_{4}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{5}\right), RM=Gr​(d​x1∧d​x2+d​x3∧d​x4)\displaystyle R_{M}=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\mathrm{d}x_{1}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{2}+\mathrm{d}x_{3}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{4}\right)

on M=ℝ5​(x1,x2,x3,x4,x5)M=\mathbb{R}^{5}(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},x_{5}) both push forward under the submersion

r:ℝ5→ℝ3,\displaystyle r:\mathbb{R}^{5}\to\mathbb{R}^{3}, r​(x1,x2,x3,x4,x5)=(x2,x3,x4)\displaystyle r(x_{1},x_{2},x_{3},x_{4},x_{5})=(x_{2},x_{3},x_{4})

to the commuting Poisson structures

r!​(LM)=Gr​(νL),\displaystyle r_{!}(L_{M})=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\nu_{L}\right), νL=∂∂x3∧∂∂x2,\displaystyle\nu_{L}=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}, r!​(RM)=Gr​(νR),\displaystyle r_{!}(R_{M})=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\nu_{R}\right), νR=∂∂x4∧∂∂x3\displaystyle\nu_{R}=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}

on P:=MK​(L​R)=ℝ3​(x2,x3,x4)P:=M_{K(LR)}=\mathbb{R}^{3}(x_{2},x_{3},x_{4}). The original Magri recipe for νL\nu_{L} and νR\nu_{R} is

EGr​(νL)=νL​(ker⁡νR)=⟨∂∂x3⟩\displaystyle E_{\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{L})}=\nu_{L}(\ker\nu_{R})=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\right\rangle

and therefore

pL′!​Gr​(νL)=T∗​PEGr​(νL)=pL′!​Gr​(νR),\displaystyle{p^{\prime}_{L}}_{!}\mathrm{Gr}\left(\nu_{L}\right)=T^{*}P_{E_{\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{L})}}={p^{\prime}_{L}}_{!}\mathrm{Gr}\left(\nu_{R}\right),

where PEGr​(νL)=ℝ2​(x2,x4)P_{E_{\mathrm{Gr}(\nu_{L})}}=\mathbb{R}^{2}(x_{2},x_{4}). On the other hand, as shown in Example 38, the reductions of LML_{M} and RMR_{M} under the Magri recipe

ELM=K​(LM⋆(RM⊛ℛ−1​(LM)))=⟨∂∂x1,∂∂x3⟩\displaystyle E_{L_{M}}=K\left(L_{M}\star\left(R_{M}\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-1}(L_{M})\right)\right)=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\right\rangle

are, respectively,

LMELM=Gr​(∂∂x5∧∂∂x4),\displaystyle L_{M_{E_{L_{M}}}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{4}}), RMELM=Gr​(∂∂x5)\displaystyle R_{M_{E_{L_{M}}}}=\mathrm{Gr}(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{5}})

on MELM=ℝ3​(x2,x4,x5)M_{E_{L_{M}}}=\mathbb{R}^{3}(x_{2},x_{4},x_{5}). Therefore the new Magri recipe for LML_{M} and RMR_{M} of Theorem 36 does not consist of applying the original Magri recipe to the Poisson structures obtained by the common kernel reduction of Theorem 30.

10.5. Comparison with the literature

We conclude the paper with a few remarks concerning the literature.

  1. I)

    The condition of involutivity along an injective immersion (Definition 1) is a version of the condition involutivity along an embedded submanifold of [23, Section 2] or [24, Appendix B2]. It seems new, and allows the discussion to happen at the generality of injective immersions, in the spirit of [13, Chapter 10].

  2. II)

    Even in the embedded case, Lemma 8 refines the criterion in [6, Theorem 3.1.1] and [21, Proposition 5.6] which ensures that the pullback i!​(L)i^{!}(L) of a Dirac structure LL is again Dirac — namely, that L∩N∗​XL\cap N^{*}X be a vector bundle:

    L∩N∗​X​VB\displaystyle L\cap N^{*}X\ \text{VB} ⟺\displaystyle\Longleftrightarrow L∩N∗​X⟂​VB\displaystyle L\cap N^{*}X^{\perp}\ \text{VB} ⟹\displaystyle\Longrightarrow L​[N∗​X]=L∩N∗​X⟂+N∗​X​VB.\displaystyle L[N^{*}X]=L\cap N^{*}X^{\perp}+N^{*}X\ \text{VB}.
  3. III)

    The characterization of the split condition is a straightforward adaptation of [25, Proposition 3.31]; see also [30, Lemma 2.15].

  4. IV)

    Various reduction schemes for Dirac structures have been proposed in the literature. The majority of the literature [14, 15, 18, 19, 20, 26, 29, 33] falls under the scope of what we call in the paper “Dirac reduction” — namely, necessary conditions under which a Dirac structure can be transferred from MM to YY. Often such procedures are termed of “Marsden-Ra t , iu kind” by their authors. As discussed in the paper, in our terminology, we advocate that only concurring reduction schemes should be regarded as of “Marsden-Ra t , iu kind”, as that is an essential feature of the original Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction in Poisson geometry, as explained later in [7, 9, 12].

  5. V)

    In [14], the authors consider the setting of a submersion p:M→Bp:M\to B, a Dirac structure LML_{M} on MM which can be pushed forward through pp, and a submanifold X⊂MX\subset M for which p:X→Y=p​(X)p:X\to Y=p(X) is still a submersion, and

    p!​i!​p!​p!​(LM)=LY\displaystyle p_{!}i^{!}p^{!}p_{!}(L_{M})=L_{Y}

    is a Dirac structure.

  6. VI)

    The simplified approach in [17] to (classical) Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction was very useful in formulating the general notion of witness, as was the observation in [19] that

    i!​(Gr​(πM)​[E])=p!​Gr​(πY).\displaystyle i^{!}\left(\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{M})[E]\right)=p^{!}\mathrm{Gr}(\pi_{Y}).

    We observe, however that the “extended Marsden-Ra t , iu reduction” of [17] moves in the direction of what we have called Dirac reduction, see e.g. [17, Proposition 4.1].

  7. VII)

    Dirac reduction (Theorem 14) is discussed in [15] and [18] in the more general situation in which the isotropic subbundle I⊂𝕋​M|XI\subset\mathbb{T}M|_{X} is allowed to be a more general isotropic subbundle, and where a background 33-form is allowed. We note that our results have a straightforward generalization to the case where LML_{M} is Dirac with respect to the Dorfman bracket twisted by a closed 3-form HM∈Ω3​(M)H_{M}\in\Omega^{3}(M),

    [u+ξ,v+η]:=[u,v]+ℒu​η−iv​d​ξ+iu∧v​HM,\displaystyle[u+\xi,v+\eta]:=[u,v]+\mathscr{L}_{u}\eta-i_{v}\mathrm{d}\xi+i_{u\wedge v}H_{M},

    provided that the pullback of HMH_{M} to XX be basic:

    (22) i∗​(HM)=p∗​(HY),\displaystyle i^{*}(H_{M})=p^{*}(H_{Y}), HY∈Ω3​(Y).\displaystyle H_{Y}\in\Omega^{3}(Y).

    Under this condition, Theorems 14 and 28 remain true in this setting with background forms. Also the recipes of Theorems 30 and 36 carry over to this setting if (22) is satisfied for the pertinent triangles.

  8. VIII)

    Our insistence that witnesses be subbundles of T​M|XTM|_{X} is directly tied to Theorem 28: if one were to replace (𝔈\mathfrak{E}) by a general isotropic subbundle of 𝕋​M|X\mathbb{T}M|_{X}, two Dirac structures LML_{M} and RMR_{M} witnessed by EE would have to satisfy additional conditions to ensure that LM⊛RML_{M}\circledast R_{M} is also witnessed by EE.

  9. IX)

    A different reduction scheme for (𝔗\mathfrak{T}) is considered in [33], in which the extra datum is given by a closed two-form ω∈Ω2​(X)\omega\in\Omega^{2}(X)555Their setting allows more generally for a background 3-form, and requires merely that p:X→Yp:X\to Y have constant rank, but we ignore that for the sake of comparison of approaches.. The Dirac reducibility condition is replaced by

    (BM) p!​ℛ−ω​i!​(LM)=LY\displaystyle p_{!}\mathcal{R}_{-\omega}i^{!}(L_{M})=L_{Y}

    for a Dirac structure LYL_{Y} on YY. The many beautiful examples in loc. cit. attest the versatility and usefulness of this reduction scheme. However, extensions of Dirac reduction of this nature do not respect concurrence, as the example below illustrates.

Example 60.

Let M=X=Y=ℝ3M=X=Y=\mathbb{R}^{3}, equipped with the Dirac structures

L=⟨∂∂x1,∂∂x2,d​x3⟩,\displaystyle L=\left\langle\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}},\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}},\mathrm{d}x_{3}\right\rangle, R=⟨x1​∂∂x3+d​x1,d​x2,x1​∂∂x1−d​x3⟩.\displaystyle R=\left\langle x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}+\mathrm{d}x_{1},\mathrm{d}x_{2},x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}-\mathrm{d}x_{3}\right\rangle.

Then

L⊛R=L\displaystyle L\circledast R=L

shows that LL and RR concur. Consider the closed two-form

ω=d​x1∧d​x2.\displaystyle\omega=\mathrm{d}x_{1}\wedge\mathrm{d}x_{2}.

Then

ℛ−ω​(L)=Gr​(∂∂x1∧∂∂x2),\displaystyle\mathcal{R}_{-\omega}(L)=\mathrm{Gr}\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}\right), ℛ−ω​(R)=Gr​(x1​∂∂x1∧∂∂x3)\displaystyle\mathcal{R}_{-\omega}(R)=\mathrm{Gr}\left(x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}\wedge\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}\right)

Because i=p=idMi=p=\mathrm{id}_{M}, this shows that both LL and RR satisfy (BM). However,

D:=ℛ−ω​(L)⊛ℛ−ω​(R)=⟨a,b,c⟩,\displaystyle D:=\mathcal{R}_{-\omega}(L)\circledast\mathcal{R}_{-\omega}(R)=\left\langle a,b,c\right\rangle,

where

a=∂∂x1−d​x2,\displaystyle a=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}-\mathrm{d}x_{2}, b=∂∂x2+x1​∂∂x3+d​x1,\displaystyle b=\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{2}}+x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{3}}+\mathrm{d}x_{1}, c:=−x1​∂∂x1+d​x3\displaystyle c:=-x_{1}\tfrac{\partial}{\partial x_{1}}+\mathrm{d}x_{3}

is not Dirac, since

ΥD​(a,b,c)=⟨[a,b],c⟩=1.\displaystyle\Upsilon_{D}(a,b,c)=\langle[a,b],c\rangle=1.

References

  • [1] Kenneth Meyer, Symmetries and integrals in mechanics, in:Dynamical systems (Proc. Sympos., Univ. Bahia, Salvador, 1971), pp. 259–272 Academic Press, New York-London (1973) doi:10.1016/B978-0-12-550350-1.50025-4
  • [2] Jerrold Marsden and Alan Weinstein, Reduction of symplectic manifolds with symmetry, Reports on mathematical physics 5, no. 1 pp. 121–130 (1974) doi:10.1016/0034-4877(74)90021-4
  • [3] Paulette Libermann, Problèmes d’équivalence et géométrie symplectique, Astérisque 107-108, pp. 43–68 (1983) http://www.numdam.org/item?id=AST_1983__107-108__43_0
  • [4] Jerrold Marsden and Tudor Ratiu, Reduction of Poisson manifolds, Lett. Math. Phys., 11 pp. 161–169 (1986) doi:10.1007/BF00398428
  • [5] Francisco-Javier Turiel, Classification locale d’un couple de formes symplectiques Poisson-compatibles, C. R. Acad. Sci. Paris, t. 308, Série I, pp. 575–578 (1989)
  • [6] Theodore James Courant, Dirac manifolds, Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. 319 (1990), 631-661 doi:10.1090/S0002-9947-1990-0998124-1
  • [7] Paolo Casati, Franco Magri and Marco Pedroni, Bihamiltonian Manifolds and τ\tau–function. In: Mathematical Aspects of Classical Field Theory 1991, M. J. Gotay et al. eds., Contemporary Mathematics 132, American Mathematical Society, Providence, RI, 1992, 213–234 doi:10.1090/conm/132
  • [8] Rui Loja Fernandes, On the master symmetries and bi-Hamiltonian structure of the Toda lattice, Journal of Physics A: Mathematical and General, Volume 26, Number 15 (1993) doi:10.1088/0305-4470/26/15/028
  • [9] Joana Nunes da Costa and Charles-Michel Marle, Reduction of bihamiltonian manifolds and recursion operators, In: Di. Geom. and Appl., Proc. Conf., Brno, Czech Republic, Aug. 1996. https://www.emis.de/proceedings/6ICDGA/index.html
  • [10] Izu Vaisman, Reduction of Poisson-Nijenhuis manifolds, Journal of Geometry and Physics Volume 19, Issue 1, 90–98 (1996) doi:10.1016/0393-0440(95)00024-0
  • [11] Zhang-Ju Liu, Alan Weinstein and Ping Xu, Manin triples for Lie bialgebroids, Journal of Differential Geometry 45, no. 3 (1997), 547–574 doi:10.4310/jdg/1214459842
  • [12] Paolo Casati, Gregorio Falqui, Franco Magri and Marco Pedroni, Bihamiltonian reductions and 𝒲n\mathcal{W}_{n} algebras, Journal of Geometry and Physics, 26, 291–310, (1998). doi:10.1016/S0393-0440(97)00060-0
  • [13] Juan-Pablo Ortega and Tudor Ratiu Momentum Maps and Hamiltonian Reduction, Progress in Mathematics, Birkhäuser Boston (2004) doi:10.1007/978-1-4757-3811-7
  • [14] Iván Calvo and Fernando Falceto, Reduction and projection of Dirac structures, Monografıas de la Real Academia de Ciencias de Zaragoza 29, 49–56 (2006)
  • [15] Henrique Bursztyn, Gil Cavalcanti and Marco Gualtieri, Reduction of Courant algebroids and generalized complex structures, Advances in Mathematics Volume 211, Issue 2, 726–765 (2007) doi:10.1016/j.aim.2006.09.008
  • [16] Alan Weinstein, The Integration Problem for Complex Lie Algebroids In: Maeda, Y., Ochiai, T., Michor, P., Yoshioka, A. (eds) From Geometry to Quantum Mechanics. Progress in Mathematics, vol 252. Birkhäuser Boston (2007) doi:10.1007/978-0-8176-4530-4_7
  • [17] Fernando Falceto and Marco Zambon, An Extension of the Marsden–Ratiu Reduction for Poisson Manifolds, Lett. Math. Phys., 85(2-3), 203–219 (2008) doi:10.1007/s11005-008-0262-7
  • [18] Marco Zambon, Reduction of branes in generalized complex geometry, Journal of Symplectic Geometry, Volume 6, Number 4, 353–378 (2008) doi:10.4310/JSG.2008.v6.n4.a1
  • [19] Iván Calvo, Fernando Falceto and Marco Zambon, Reduction of Dirac structures along isotropic subbundles, Reports on Mathematical Physics 65, no. 2, 259–269 (2010) doi:10.1016/S0034-4877(10)80020-5
  • [20] Jedrzej Śniatycki, Reduction of symmetries of Dirac structures, J. Fixed Point Theory Appl. 10 (2011) 339–358 doi:10.1007/s11784-011-0063-y
  • [21] Henrique Bursztyn, A brief introduction to Dirac manifolds, in: Geometric and topological methods for quantum field theory, 4–38, Cambridge Univ. Press, Cambridge, 2013. doi:10.1017/CBO9781139208642.002
  • [22] Olivier Brahic, Rui Loja Fernandes, Integrability and reduction of Hamiltonian actions on Dirac manifolds, Indagationes Mathematicæ, v. 25 (2014), p. 901–925 doi:10.1016/j.indag.2014.07.007
  • [23] David Li-Bland, Pseudo-Dirac structures, Indagationes MathematicæVolume 25, Issue 5, 1054–1101 (2014) doi:10.1016/j.indag.2014.07.010
  • [24] Henrique Bursztyn, Alejandro Cabrera and Matias del Hoyo, Vector bundles over Lie groupoids and algebroids, Advances in Mathematics Volume 290, 163–207 (2016) doi:10.1016/j.aim.2015.11.044
  • [25] Eckhard Meinrenken, Introduction to Poisson Geometry, lecture notes (2017) https://www.math.toronto.edu/mein/teaching/MAT1341_PoissonGeometry/Poisson8.pdf
  • [26] Pedro Frejlich and Ioan Mărcuţ, On dual pairs in Dirac geometry, Math. Z. 289, 171–200 (2018). doi:10.1007/s00209-017-1947-3
  • [27] Eckhard Meinrenken, Poisson geometry from a Dirac perspective Lett Math Phys 108, 447–498 (2018) doi:10.1007/s11005-017-0977-4
  • [28] Henrique Bursztyn, Thiago Drummond, and Clarice Netto, Dirac structures and Nijenhuis operators, Math. Z. 302, 875–915 (2022). doi:10.1007/s00209-022-03078-5
  • [29] Alejandro Cabrera and Cristian Ortiz, Quotients of multiplicative forms and Poisson reduction, Differential Geometry and its Applications 83 (2022): 101898 doi:10.1016/j.difgeo.2022.101898
  • [30] Lilian Brambila, Pedro Frejlich and David Martínez Torres, Coregular submanifolds and Poisson submersions, Revista Matemática Iberoamericana, Volume 40, Issue 4, 1419–1468, (2024) doi:10.4171/RMI/1464
  • [31] Dan Aguero, On the geometry of complex Poisson bivectors (2025) arXiv:2506.18097
  • [32] Pedro Frejlich and David Martínez Torres, Dirac products and concurring Dirac structures, Lett Math Phys 115, 45 (2025). doi:10.1007/s11005-025-01936-x
  • [33] Ana Balibanu, Maxence Mayrand, Reduction along strong Dirac maps, Proceedings of the London Mathematical Society, Volume 132, Issue 2 (2026) doi:10.1112/plms.70123
  • [34] Pedro Frejlich and David Martínez Torres, On the Nijenhuis condition for Dirac structures, in preparation
Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
